documents.CP-001: Canada Accessibility Policy Afterpay Last Approved: Dec 8, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 8, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Canada Accessibility Policy ("Policy") outlines Afterpay Canada Limited ("Afterpay Canada") approach to providing an inclusive and accessible environment for all Staff Members. This Policy outlines Afterpay Canada's commitment to ensuring accessibility in accordance with federal and provincial legislation in Canada. The Head of Compliance, Afterpay Canada is responsible for overseeing this Policy. Afterpay Canada conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations Please refer to the Section: Related Documentation or References of this Policy). The purpose of this Policy is to further Block's accessibility practices process by ensuring that Afterpay Canada provides an inclusive and accessible environment for all Staff Members. This Policy outlines Afterpay Canada's commitment to identifying, removing, and preventing barriers to accessibility in accordance with federal and provincial legislation in Canada. Scope: This Policy is established by Afterpay Canada and is applicable to all Afterpay Canada business lines, and jurisdictions within Canada. It outlines the standards and practices to ensure accessibility for all Staff Members. Specific Exclusions: This Policy does not apply to products or services distributed by third parties, external bank partners, or any subsidiaries operating outside of Canada. Definitions: Terminology Definition Staff Member(s) References to "Staff Member(s)" throughout this document shall refer to all full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers including interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1 Commitment to Accessibility Afterpay Canada will perform the following on a continual basis: 1. Identifying and Removing Barriers: Identifying barriers to accessibility and implementing measures to remove these barriers; 2. Preventing Barriers: Proactively preventing the creation of new barriers to accessibility; 3. Inclusive Environment: Ensuring Afterpay Canada's services, communications, and customer interactions are inclusive and accessible to all. 2.2 Federal Legislation Accessible Canada Act, SC 2019, c 10 The Accessible Canada Act (“ACAˮ) is a federal law that aims to make Canada barrier-free by 2040. It requires organizations under federal jurisdiction to identify, remove, and prevent barriers in various areas, including information and communication technologies, the built environment, and the delivery of programs and services. 2.3 Provincial and Territorial Regulations Ontario; Accessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act, 2005 (“AODAˮ) This Act aims to improve accessibility across Ontario. Afterpay Canada will ensure compliance with the following: ● Accessible Feedback: Feedback processes must be accessible to persons with disabilities. Afterpay Canada will provide accessible formats and communication support upon request. Manitoba; The Accessibility for Manitobans Act, SM 2013, c 40 (“AMAˮ) This Act aims to eliminate barriers and improve accessibility in Manitoba. Afterpay Canada's compliance includes: ● Accessible Feedback: Feedback processes will be accessible, with public notifications about these options provided on Afterpay Canada's website and feedback forms. ● Accessible Communication Formats and Support: Afterpay Canada will provide accessible formats and communication supports promptly and without extra cost, after consulting with the requester. ● Customer Service Standard: Implementing policies will ensure the dignity, independence, and integration of persons with disabilities in Afterpay Canada's service delivery, providing equal opportunities for access. Nova Scotia; Accessibility Act, 2017, SNS 2017, c 2 This Act aims to make Nova Scotia accessible by 2030. Afterpay Canada will comply by providing: ● Accessible Feedback: Ensuring feedback

documents.CP-004: Canada's Anti-Spam Legislation CASL Policy Afterpay Last Approved: Dec 8, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 8, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Canada Anti-Spam Legislation (“CASLˮ) Policy ("Policy") outlines Afterpay Canada Limited ("Afterpay Canada") approach to combat spam and regulate electronic communications in Canada. The Head of Compliance, Afterpay Canada, is responsible for overseeing this Policy. This Policy should be read in connection with Block's CANSPAM Policy and if there is any conflict between Block and the applicable local law, this Policy must be followed. Afterpay Canada conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations in accordance with CASL. The purpose of this Policy is to further Block's protection of Canadians from unsolicited electronic messages while fostering trust and confidence in digital communications. It applies to a wide range of electronic messages sent for commercial purposes, including emails, text messages, and other electronic messaging. CASL not only addresses spam but also governs the installation of computer programs and the alteration of transmission data. This legislation is crucial for Afterpay Canada as it operates within the digital payment services sector, where electronic communications play a vital role in customer engagement and business operations. In short, CASL aims to: ● Protect individuals and businesses from unsolicited electronic messages, spyware, and malware. ● Regulate CEMs to ensure transparency, accountability, and trust in digital communications. ● Facilitate a secure and competitive digital marketplace in Canada. Scope: This Policy is established by Afterpay Canada and is applicable to all Afterpay business lines and jurisdictions within Canada. It applies to Staff Members involved in the marketing, distribution, and communication activities of Afterpay Canada. This Policy does not apply to: ● Products and services that are distributed by third-party partners outside the jurisdiction of Canada. ● Bank partners who are independently responsible for their compliance with CASL. ● Any business activities or communications managed by external marketing agencies that operate under their own CASL compliance policies. Definitions: Terminology Definition CASL Canada Anti-Spam Legislation (“CASLˮ) protects consumers and businesses from the misuse of digital technology, including spam and other electronic threats. CEMs Commercial Electronic Messages (“CEMˮs) are any Email messages the Primary Purpose of which is to advertise or promote for a commercial purpose, a commercial product or service including content on the Internet. An email message would not be considered to be a CEM solely because such a message includes a reference to a commercial entity that serves to identify the Sender or a reference or link to an Internet website operated for a commercial purpose. Sender A person who initiates an email message and whose product, service, or internet website is advertised or promoted by the message. Staff Member(s) References to "Staff Member(s)" throughout this document shall refer to all full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1 Who CASL Protects CASL provides protection to all individuals, businesses, and organizations in Canada from unwanted electronic communications. This includes consumers, businesses, and government entities. CASL's reach extends beyond Canadian borders to anyone sending electronic messages to or from Canada, emphasizing its global impact on digital communications. 2.2 Information requirements in Commercial Electronic Messages CEMs When sending CEMs, Afterpay Canada must include certain information to comply with CASL. This includes: ● Identifying information about the Sender, including name and contact information. ● A clear and functional unsubscribe mechanism that allows recipients t
documents.CP-005: Controlling the Assault of Non-Solicited Pornography and Marketing Act (“CANSPAMˮ) Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The Controlling the Assault of Non-Solicited Pornography And Marketing Act (“CANSPAMˮ) Policy demonstrates Block, Inc.'s commitment to comply with the CANSPAM Act across all its subsidiaries and operations. This policy establishes comprehensive guidelines governing Block's commercial email practices while maintaining brand-specific flexibility where needed. The CANSPAM Act serves as a crucial regulatory framework enacted to reduce spam and unsolicited pornography by prohibiting senders of unsolicited commercial email messages from disguising the source and content of their messages, while ensuring consumers maintain control over their email communications. CANSPAM requires Block to follow specific procedures when initiating email messages to customers or prospective customers, including obtaining affirmative consent, ensuring message content is not false or misleading, and providing recipients with the ability to opt-out of future correspondence. Scope: This Policy applies to Block, Inc. and its applicable U.S. subsidiaries and affiliates (collectively, "Block") that send commercial email messages. The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. It governs all commercial emails sent by or on behalf of Block, including messages with both commercial and transactional content, and third-party email services. This Policy applies to Square, Cash App, and Afterpay's commercial email communications but does not apply to purely transactional messages or internal communications. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Affirmative As it relates to commercial electronic mail ("Email") messages, Affirmative Consent Consent occurs when the Recipient expressly consents to receive the message, either in response to a clear and conspicuous request for such consent or at the Recipient's own initiative. If the message is from a party other than the party to which the Recipient communicated such consent, the Recipient must have been given clear and conspicuous notice at the time the consent was communicated that their Email address could be transferred to such other party for the purpose of initiating Commercial Email Messages. Commercial Any Email message the Primary Purpose of which is to advertise or promote Email Message for a commercial purpose, a commercial product or service including content on the Internet. An Email message would not be considered to be a Commercial Email Message solely because such a message includes a reference to a commercial entity that serves to identify the Sender or a reference or link to an Internet website operated for a commercial purpose. Primary Purpose The Primary Purpose of an Email message will be deemed to be commercial if it: ● Contains only the commercial advertisement or promotion of a commercial product or service ● Contains both commercial content and "transactional or relationship" content if either: ○ A Recipient reasonably interpreting the subject line of the Email message would likely conclude that the message contains commercial content; or ○ The Email message's "transactional or relationship" content does not appear in whole or substantial part at the beginning of the body of the message ● Contains both commercial content as well as content that is not transactional or relationship content if a Recipient reasonably interprets either: ○ The subject line of the Email message would likely conclude that the message contains commercial content; or ○ The body of the message would likely conclude that the Primary Purpose of the message is commercial Recipient An authorized user of the Email address to which the message was sent or delivered. 2 Sende
documents.CP-006: Electronic Fund Transfer Act Regulation E Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Electronic Fund Transfer Act Policy (“Policyˮ) outlines Block, Inc.'s U.S. commitment to adhering to regulatory requirements as well as industry standards and best practices in the delivery of its products and services. This Policy is applicable in the United States across all products that involve Electronic Fund Transfers (“EFTˮ) and Remittance Transfers as those transactions are defined under the Electronic Fund Transfer Act (“EFTAˮ) 15 U.S.C. §§ 1693 et seq.) and its implementing regulation, Regulation E 12 C.F.R. Part 1005. This Policy establishes comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc. and its U.S. subsidiaries' electronic fund transfer services while ensuring consistent consumer protections across all platforms. The purpose of the EFTA and Regulation E is to establish the basic rights, liabilities, and responsibilities of Consumers who use EFT and Remittance Transfer services and of the financial institutions or other persons that offer these services. The primary objective of the EFTA and Regulation E is the protection of individual Consumers engaging in EFTs and Remittance Transfers. Scope: This Policy applies to Block, Inc. and its subsidiaries (collectively, "Block") in the United States, governing all activities involving EFTs and Remittance Transfers subject to EFTA and Regulation E. The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition A card, code, login credentials or other means of access to a Consumer’s Access Device account, or any combination of devices, which may be used by the Consumer to initiate an EFT. This could be the Consumer's debit card, phone, computer/laptop, or login information. Anything that Consumers use to access their funds. A demand deposit (checking), savings, or other consumer asset account Account (other than an occasional or incidental credit balance in a credit plan) held directly or indirectly by a financial institution and established primarily for personal, family, or household purposes. The term includes a Prepaid Account. An individual who is a natural person who has obtained an Account that is Consumer to be used primarily for personal, family or household purposes. Any transfer of funds that is initiated through an Electronic Terminal, Electronic Fund telephone, computer, or magnetic tape for the purpose of ordering, instructing, Transfer (“EFTˮ) or authorizing a financial institution to debit or credit a Consumer's Account. The term includes, but is not limited to: ● Point-of-sale transfers; ● Automated teller machine transfers; ● Direct deposits or withdrawals of funds; ● Transfers initiated by telephone; and ● Transfers resulting from debit card transactions, whether or not initiated through an Electronic Terminal. An electronic device other than a telephone operated by a Consumer, Electronic through which a Consumer may initiate an EFT, including point-of-sale Terminal terminals, ATMs and cash dispensing machines. The term Error means: Error ● Unauthorized EFTs; ● Incorrect or missing transfers to or from a Consumer’s account; ● Omission of a transaction from a periodic statement; ● Computational or bookkeeping Errors; ● Receipt of an incorrect amount from an Electronic Terminal; and ● Requests for documentation (unless requesting a duplicate copy), or requests for information concerning a transaction or clarification to determine whether an “Error” specified above exists. documents.CP-007: Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (“ESignˮ) Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act Policy ("Policy") outlines Block, Inc.'s enterprise-wide approach to compliance with the Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act ("ESign"). The purpose of this Policy is to establish comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc. and its U.S. subsidiaries' use of electronic signatures and records, ensuring consistent operations while maintaining brand-specific flexibility. Scope: This Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act Policy (the "Policy") outlines Block, Inc.'s enterprise-wide approach to compliance with the Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act ("ESign"). This Policy establishes comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc. and its U.S. subsidiaries' use of electronic signatures and records, ensuring consistent operations while maintaining brand-specific flexibility. The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. Definitions: Terminology Definition Consumer An individual who obtains or has obtained a financial product or service that is to be used primarily for personal, family, or household purposes. Electronic Relating to technology having electrical, digital, magnetic, wireless, optical, electromagnetic, or similar capabilities. Electronic A contract or other record created, generated, sent, communicated, Record received, or stored by electronic means. 1 Electronic An electronic sound, symbol, or process attached to or logically associated Signature with a contract or other record and executed or adopted by a person with the intent to sign the record. Record Information that is inscribed on a tangible medium or that is stored in an electronic or other medium and is retrievable in perceivable form. Transaction An action or set of actions relating to the conduct of business, consumer, or commercial affairs between two or more persons. Staff Member(s) All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support Block's operations, including interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1 Fundamental ESign Requirements Block recognizes that electronic signatures and records form the foundation of modern digital commerce. To ensure the validity and enforceability of electronic transactions, Block implements four essential requirements across all its operations: 1 clear demonstration of intent to sign, 2 informed consent and demonstrated ability to access electronic records, 3 reliable association between signatures and signers, and 4 secure record retention. These requirements enable Block to provide information and notices electronically while maintaining compliance with all applicable regulations. ESign serves as an overlay to existing laws and regulations, ensuring that signatures, contracts, or other records relating to a transaction maintain their validity when executed electronically. This framework allows Block to facilitate efficient electronic commerce while preventing deception and fraud in electronic transactions. Block achieves this through comprehensive systems and controls that verify, record, and maintain electronic transactions in a manner that meets or exceeds regulatory standards. 2.2 Consumer Notification and Transparency Before initiating electronic communications, Block ensures transparent communication with Consumers about the electronic relationship. Block provides clear and conspicuous information about the scope of electronic communications, whether for a specific transaction or ongoing relationship. documents.CP-008: Equal Credit Opportunity Act (“ECOAˮ) Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Equal Credit Opportunity Act Policy ("Policy") outlines Block's enterprise-wide approach to ensuring fair and equitable lending practices across its credit products. The purpose of this Policy is to establish comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc., and its U.S. subsidiaries' compliance with the Equal Credit Opportunity Act ("ECOA"), its implementing Regulation B, and related state laws, while ensuring consistent operations and maintaining brand-specific flexibility, where applicable. Block's Business Product Compliance Lead, Lending & Credit, and/or their designee, is responsible for the management of this Policy. Scope: This Policy applies to all Credit Products offered by Block, Inc., its subsidiaries and affiliates (collectively, "Block") in the United States whether consumer or commercial purpose, as ECOA protects both individual and business credit applicants. The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. Definitions: Terminology Definition Adverse Action A refusal to grant credit in substantially the amount or on substantially the terms requested in an Application unless (a) Block makes a counteroffer (to grant credit in a different amount or on other terms) and the Applicant uses or expressly accepts the credit offered; (b) A termination of an account or an unfavorable change in the terms of an account that does not affect all or substantially all of a class of the creditor's accounts; or (c) A refusal to increase the amount of credit available to an applicant who has made an application for an increase. Adverse Action does not include a change in the terms of an account expressly agreed to by an Applicant, or any action or forbearance relating to an account taken in connection with inactivity, default, or delinquency as to that account. 1 Applicant A person (individual or entity) that requests credit or who has received credit from Block, including any person that may become contractually liable for credit provided by Block. Application An oral or written request for an extension of credit made in accordance with procedures used by Block for the type of credit requested. Completed An application that Block has received which contains all the information that Application Block regularly obtains and considers in evaluating applications for the amount and type of credit requested (including, but not limited to, credit reports, any additional information requested from the applicant, and any approvals or reports by governmental agencies or other persons that are necessary to guarantee, insure, or provide security for the credit or collateral). Credit Products Cash App and Afterpay branded consumer loan products for personal, family, or household purposes (including, but not limited to, Cash App Borrow, Cash App Afterpay Pay Over Time, Afterpay on Cash App Card, Cash App Afterpay Online Checkout, Afterpay Pay-in-4, and Afterpay Pay Monthly) and Squareʼs business-purpose credit products. Prohibited Basis ● Race or color ● Religion ● National origin ● Sex ● Marital status ● Age, as long as the applicant is able to contract ● Receipt of income from a public assistance program ● Exercising rights under the Consumer Credit Protection Act in good faith ● Gender/gender identity/gender expression ● Sexual Orientation ● Veteran/servicemember status ● Disability ● Pregnancy ● HIV Status ● Ancestry 2. documents.CP-009: Fair & Responsible Lending Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. It is important to note that this Policy does not include fair lending components that apply exclusively to Model Risk Management (“MRMˮ), which are governed separately. ECOAʼs implementing Regulation B, 12 CFR Part 1002, elaborates on these and other principles and requirements for fair and non-discriminatory access to credit throughout the credit lifecycle. documents.CP-010: Fair Credit Reporting Act (“FCRAˮ) Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Fair Credit Reporting Act Policy (the "Policy") outlines Block, Inc.'s enterprise-wide approach to consumer credit reporting, information handling practices, and identity theft prevention measures. The purpose of this Policy is to establish comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc. and its U.S. subsidiaries' compliance with the Fair Credit Reporting Act (“FCRAˮ) and its amendment, the Fair and Accurate Credit Transactions Act (“FACTAˮ), ensuring consistent operations while maintaining brand-specific flexibility, where applicable. Block's Business Product Compliance Lead, Lending & Credit, and/or their designee, is responsible for the management of this Policy. Scope: This Policy applies to all Credit Products offered by Block, Inc., its subsidiaries and affiliates (collectively, "Block"), The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. Definitions: Terminology Definition Adverse Action A denial or revocation of credit, a change in existing credit terms, or a refusal to grant credit in substantially the amount or terms requested. This includes decisions made using information from consumer reports. Consumer Any individual who obtains or seeks to obtain financial products or services primarily for personal, family, or household purposes. Consumer Report Any written, oral, or other communication by a consumer reporting agency bearing on a consumer's creditworthiness, credit standing, credit capacity, character, general reputation, personal characteristics, or mode of living used for credit decisions. 1 Credit Products Cash App- and Afterpay-branded consumer loan products for personal, family, or household purposes (including, but not limited to, Cash App Borrow, Cash App Afterpay Pay Over Time, Afterpay on Cash App Card, Cash App Afterpay Online Checkout, Afterpay Pay-in-4, and Afterpay Pay Monthly) and Squareʼs business-purpose credit products. Credit Score A numerical value derived from a statistical tool or modeling system used to predict credit behavior. Identity Theft A fraud committed using another person's identifying information without authority. Blockʼs permissible purposes for obtaining a consumer report are 1 to use the information in connection with a credit transaction involving the consumer on whom the information is to be furnished and involving the extension of credit to, or review or collection of an account of, the consumer; or 2 to review an account to determine whether the consumer continues to meet the terms of the account. These permissible purposes primarily arise in credit transactions where Block has received explicit authorization from the consumer, either in writing or electronically. This includes situations where the report will support credit-related activities such as extending new credit, reviewing existing accounts, or supporting collection activities. Additionally, Block may obtain consumer reports when working with potential investors or servicers who need to assess credit risks, or in connection with prescreened credit solicitations where the consumer has provided authorization or the transaction constitutes a firm offer of credit and the consumer has not opted out. documents.CP-011: Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (“FDCPAˮ) Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Fair Debt Collection Practices Act Policy (the "Policy") outlines Block, Inc.'s enterprise-wide approach to collections. The purpose of this Policy is to establish comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc., and its U.S. subsidiaries' utilization of the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act “FDCPAˮ) framework, ensuring consistent operations while maintaining brand-specific flexibility, where applicable. Block's Business Product Compliance Lead, Lending & Credit, and/or their designee, is responsible for the management of this Policy. This Policy establishes comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc., and its U.S. subsidiaries' approach to debt collection practices, ensuring consistent operations while maintaining brand-specific flexibility. Scope: This Policy applies to all Credit Products offered by Block, Inc., its subsidiaries, and affiliates (collectively, "Block"), in the United States, including, but not limited to installment loans, buy-now, pay-later products, and other forms of consumer lending. The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. While Block and its subsidiaries do not meet the definition of a Debt Collector under the FDCPA, Block has elected to implement FDCPA guidelines as a best practice when collecting debts or engaging third-party debt collection agencies that may collect debts on behalf of Block. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Attempt to Any act to initiate a communication or other contact about a Debt with Communicate any person through any medium, including by soliciting a response from such person. An attempt to communicate includes leaving a limited-content message. Consumer Any natural person, whether living or deceased, obligated or allegedly obligated to pay any Debt. Creditor Any person who offers or extends credit creating a Debt or to whom a Debt is owed. The term Creditor does not include any person who receives an assignment or transfer of a Debt in default solely to facilitate collection of the Debt for another. Credit Products Cash App and Afterpay branded consumer loan products for personal, family, or household purposes (including, but not limited to, Cash App Borrow, Cash App Afterpay Pay Over Time, Afterpay on Cash App Card, Cash App Afterpay Online Checkout, Afterpay Pay-in-4, and Afterpay Pay Monthly) and Squareʼs business-purpose credit products. Debt Any obligation or alleged obligation of a Consumer to pay money arising out of a transaction in which the money, property, insurance, or services are primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, whether or not the obligation has been reduced to judgment. Debt Collector Any person who regularly collects or attempts to collect, directly or indirectly, consumer debts for another person or institution or uses a name other than its own when collecting its own consumer debts. 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1 General Policy Requirements Block is committed to ensuring that collection activity across all Credit Products is conducted in a manner consistent with the FDCPA. The primary purposes of this Policy are to eliminate abusive, deceptive, and unfair debt collection practices, promote fair debt collection, provide consumers with an avenue for disputing and obtaining validation of debt information, and promote consistent state action to protect consumers against debt collection abuses. 2 Where state or local regulations overlap and are stricter than the requirements set out in this Policy, the more conservative approach will be applied. If any applicable requirements conflict with this Policy, Block will consult with appropriate legal counsel to resolve the conflict and adjust policies and procedures t

By conforming with these requirements Block is permitted to provide consumers with information and certain notices electronically. 2.2 Consumer Notification Prior to obtaining the consumer's consent to electronic records and disclosures, Block must clearly and conspicuously inform the consumer of several key elements. This includes an explanation of whether the consent applies only to a particular transaction that triggered the disclosure or to specific categories of records that may be provided electronically or made available as part of the consumer relationship, such as disclosures, consents, policies, change-in-terms, and adverse action notices. documents.CP-014: Military Lending Act (“MLAˮ) Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Military Lending Act Policy (the "Policy") outlines Block's enterprise-wide approach to compliance with the Military Lending Act ("MLA"). The purpose of this Policy is to establish comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc., and its U.S. subsidiaries' compliance with the MLA, ensuring consistent operations while maintaining brand-specific flexibility, where applicable. Block's Business Product Compliance Lead, Lending & Credit, and/or their designee, is responsible for the management of this Policy. Scope: This Policy applies to all Consumer Credit products offered by Block, Inc., its subsidiaries, and affiliates (collectively, "Block") in the United States, including, but not limited to installment loans, buy-now, pay-later products, and other forms of consumer lending. The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. Definitions: Terminology Definition Consumer Credit Credit offered or extended to a Covered Borrower primarily for personal, family, or household purposes. Creditor A creditor under the MLA is a person who is: ● Engaged in the business of extending consumer credit; or ● An assignee of a person engaged in the business of extending consumer credit with respect to any consumer credit extended. Covered A consumer who, at the time the consumer becomes obligated on a Borrower Consumer Credit transaction or establishes an account for Consumer Credit, is a Covered Member of the armed forces or a Dependent of a Covered Member. Covered Members of the Army, Navy, Marine Corps, Air Force, or Coast Guard Member currently serving on active duty under a call or order that does not specify a period of 30 days or fewer, or such a member serving on Active Guard and Reserve duty. Dependent With respect to a Covered Member, a Dependent means: ● The Covered Member's spouse ● Children under the age of 21 ● Children under the age of 23 enrolled in an accredited institution of higher learning who are dependent on the Covered Member for over half of their support ● Children of any age that are incapable of self-support. Other relationships may qualify as Dependents, and employees should consult compliance. Military Annual The cost of the Consumer Credit expressed as an annual rate, calculated in Percentage Rate accordance with 32 CFR 232.4(c). The MAPR for covered transactions must (“MAPRˮ) not exceed 36 percent. Active Service Also known as "active duty" or "active status." A person who is on active duty in the military full-time for a period of greater than 30 days. They work for the military full-time, may live on a military base, and can be deployed at any time. Such term includes full-time training duty, annual training duty, and attendance, while in active military service, at a school designated as a service school by law or by the Secretary of Defense. 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1 Policy Statement The standards set out in this Policy represent minimum requirements based on applicable legal and regulatory guidance and apply throughout Block's lending operations. These requirements are intended to prevent Block and its employees from violating applicable provisions of the MLA. Block recognizes that MLA violations can present significant legal, reputational, and compliance risk. 2.2 MLA Policy Requirements Military Annual Percentage Rate All transactions covered by the MLA are subject to the Military Annual Percentage Rate ("MAPR"). The MAPR is analogous to the Annual Percentage Rate ("APR") as defined in other consumer protection regulations in that both are calculated in the same manner. However, the MAPR calculation must include certain fees and charges regardless of whether other regulations require those fees or charges

Military Service Includes: ● Active Duty members of Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, Space Force, or Coast Guard; ● Federal Active Duty Reservists; ● National Guard on active service for 30 consecutive days for national emergency; ● Commissioned Officers of the Public Health Service or National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration on active service; ● Absences due to sickness, wounds, leave, or other lawful cause; and ● U.S. citizens serving with allied forces. Period of Military The period beginning the date on which a servicemember enters military Service service, and ending the date on which the servicemember is released from military service or dies while in military service. Servicemember Full-time members of uniformed services, including: ● Army, Navy, Air Force, Marines, Coast Guard, Space Force; ● Public Health Services Officers; ● NOAA commissioned corps; ● Active Duty Reserves; and ● National Gu
documents.CP-016: Telephone Consumer Protection Act (“TCPAˮ) Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Telephone Consumer Protection Act ("TCPA") Policy ("Policy") outlines Block's obligations for adhering to the TCPA as it pertains to Block's operations. The TCPA restricts and limits how Block ("Company") or a person or entity acting on behalf of Block can contact telephone subscribers via facsimile, telephone, including artificial and prerecorded voice, or message. In general, calls, messages, or facsimiles subject to the TCPA require the recipient's consent. The type and nature of consent required depends on whether the call or text message is placed for Telemarketing purposes or for "informational" purposes. Block has chosen to adhere to certain provisions of the TCPA related to its communication practices across its operations to ensure consistent Called Party protection even though Block asserts that the TCPA may not apply to Blockʼs practices as a matter of law. Block conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Policy is to demonstrate the commitment of the Company to comply with the applicable requirements of the TCPA. It has been created to ensure that Block and relevant personnel are aware of and comply with any applicable obligations under the TCPA. Scope: This Policy is established for Block, Inc. and is applicable to the Block, Inc. business and related products and services. The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. This Policy outlines the requirements for adhering to TCPA as they pertain to Block, Inc. and relevant personnel. The TCPA restricts and limits how entities can contact Called Parties via facsimile, telephone, including landline and wireless numbers, and text message. In general, calls, messages, or facsimiles subject to the TCPA require the recipient’s consent. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Advertisement Any material advertising the commercial availability or quality of any property, good, or service. Automatic Telephone Dialing System ("ATDS") Equipment that has the capacity to store or produce telephone numbers to be called, using a random or sequential number generator, and to dial such numbers. Called Party The current subscriber or customary user of the telephone number being contacted via telephone, message, or facsimile. Established Business Relationship For purposes of Telephone Solicitations, the term means a prior or existing relationship formed by a voluntary two-way communication between a Block brand (e.g. Square, Cash App, Afterpay, SFS, etc.) or a person or entity acting on behalf of that Block brand and a Called Party, with or without an exchange of consideration, on the basis of the Called Party's purchase or transaction with the Block brand within the 18 months immediately preceding the date of the telephone call, or on the basis of the Called Party's inquiry or application regarding products or services offered by the Block brand within the three months immediately preceding the date of the call, which relationship has not been previously terminated by either party. A Called Party's presence on Block's Internal Do-Not-Call list terminates the Established Business Relationship for the purpose of telephone solicitations. For purposes of facsimile Advertisements, the term means a prior or existing relationship formed by a voluntary two-way communication between a Block 2 Terminology Definition brand or a person or entity acting on behalf of a Block brand and a Called Party, with or without an exchange of consideration, on the basis of an inquiry, application, purchase, or transaction by the Called Party regarding products offered by Block, which relationship has not been previously terminated by either party.

Billing Cycle The interval between the days or dates of regular periodic statements. These intervals must be equal and no longer than a quarter of a year. Periodic Rate A rate of finance charge that is or may be imposed by a creditor on a balance for a day, week, month, or other subdivision of a year. Finance Charge The cost of Consumer credit is represented as a dollar amount, including any charge payable directly or indirectly by the Consumer and imposed directly or indirectly by Block as a condition of the credit. 2. The disclosures will be provided to the Consumer in a reasonably understandable form and may be provided electronically, subject to compliance with the Consumerʼs consent and other applicable provisions of the Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (“ESign Actˮ). 2 Content of Disclosures The disclosures will reflect the credit terms to which the parties will be legally bound as of the outset of the credit transaction. documents.CP-018: Block, Inc. UDAAP/UDAP Policy Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Policy communicates Block, Inc.'s commitment to compliance with both consumer and commercial fairness requirements through: ● The Unfair, Deceptive, or Abusive Acts or Practices (“UDAAPˮ) Standards contained in sections 1031 and 1036 of the Dodd-Frank Act ● The Unfair or Deceptive Acts or Practices (“UDAPˮ) provisions in Section 5 of the Federal Trade Commission Act ● State laws prohibiting similar acts or practices in both consumer and commercial contexts This Policy sets forth comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc. and its U.S. subsidiaries, ensuring consistent operations while maintaining appropriate flexibility to address unique business needs. Block's prevention of UDAAP and UDAP violations requires transparency, truthfulness, suitability, and fairness across all transactions and dealings with both consumers and merchants. The distinction between UDAAP and UDAP requirements reflects the different contexts in which Block operates: ● Consumer financial products and services (subject to UDAAP under Dodd-Frank) ● Commercial and merchant services (subject to UDAP under the FTC Act) ● Products with potential dual consumer and commercial applications This comprehensive approach ensures appropriate protections while recognizing the different needs and sophistication levels of consumers and commercial customers. Scope: This Policy applies to Block, Inc. and its U.S. subsidiaries and affiliates (collectively, "Block") and covers: ● Consumer Financial Products and Services UDAAP Block's consumer-focused subsidiaries Cash App and Afterpay) must comply with Unfair, Deceptive, or Abusive Acts or Practices requirements under sections 1031 and 1036 of the Dodd-Frank Act. 1 The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. Block maintains appropriate controls for preventing unfair or deceptive practices across all business lines, while applying additional consumer protection requirements where applicable. This Policy establishes requirements for all customer interactions, whether consumer or commercial, while recognizing the different regulatory frameworks that apply to each business context. Definitions: Terminology Definition Commercial A transaction primarily for business, commercial, or agricultural purposes, Transaction including merchant services and business lending products. UDAAP Unfair, Deceptive, or Abusive Acts or Practices as prohibited by sections 1031 and 1036 of the Dodd-Frank Act, which apply to consumer financial products and services. UDAP Unfair or Deceptive Acts or Practices as prohibited by Section 5 of the Federal Trade Commission Act, which apply to both consumer and commercial transactions. Unfair An act or practice that: 1 causes or is likely to cause substantial injury to consumers or merchants; 2 cannot be reasonably avoided; and 3 is not outweighed by countervailing benefits to consumers/merchants or to competition. Deceptive A representation, omission, act or practice that: 1 misleads or is likely to mislead the consumer or merchant; 2 the consumer's or merchant's interpretation is reasonable under the circumstances; and 3 the misleading representation, omission, act or practice is material. Abusive An act or practice that: 1 materially interferes with the ability of a consumer to understand a term or condition of a consumer financial product or service; or 2 takes unreasonable advantage of: (a) a consumer's lack of understanding, (b) inability to protect their interests, or (c) reasonable reliance on a covered person. Applies only to consumer transactions] Consumer An individual who is a natural person who obtains or has obtained a financial product or service that is used primarily for personal, fa

Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Lending Identity Theft Prevention/Red Flags Standard (the “Standardˮ) describes the principles, practices, and guidelines for the management and oversight of identity theft compliance risk for Cash Appʼs US Consumer Lending products (“Consumer Lendingˮ). This Standard is designed to ensure that elements of an effective Identity Theft Prevention Program (“Programˮ) are met, and that the Program is appropriate for the size and complexity of Cash App and the nature and scope of the Consumer Lending activities. Scope: This Standard applies to all consumer-purpose Credit Products offered by Cash App. Definitions: Terminology Definition Account A continuing relationship established by a person with a financial institution or creditor to obtain a product or service for personal, family, household or business purposes. Board of In the case of a branch or agency of a foreign bank, the managing official in Directors charge of the branch or agency; and in the case of any other creditor that does not have a board of directors, a designated employee at the level of senior management. Covered An account that a financial institution or creditor offers or maintains, primarily Account for personal, family, or household purposes, that involves or is designed to permit multiple payments or transactions, such as a credit card account, mortgage loan, automobile loan, general consumer loan, margin account, cell 1 phone account, utility account, checking account, or savings account; and any other account that the financial institution or creditor offers or maintains for which there is a reasonably foreseeable risk to customers or to the safety and soundness of the financial institution or creditor from identity theft, including financial, operational, compliance, reputation, or litigation risks. Credit Products Cash App-branded consumer loan products for personal, family, or household purposes (including, but not limited to, Cash App Borrow, Cash App Afterpay Pay Over Time, Afterpay on Cash Card, Afterpay Pay-in-4, Afterpay Pay Monthly). Customer A person that has a covered account with a financial institution or creditor. Identity Theft Fraud committed or attempted fraud using one or more pieces of identifying information of another person without authority. Identifying information means any name or number that may be used, alone or in conjunction with any other information, to identify a specific person, including, but not limited to, any name, social security number, or date of birth. Red Flag A pattern, practice, or specific activity that indicates the possible existence of identity theft. Service Provider The entity or business that provides a service directly to the financial institution or creditor. 2. Cash App Requirements 2.1. Identity Theft Prevention Program Administration In order to mitigate Identity Theft, Cash App will establish a Program that 1 receives initial approval from either the Board of Directors, or an appropriate governing committee; 2 involves a designated employee in the oversight, development, implementation and administration of the Program; 3 trains employees, as necessary, to effectively implement the Program; and 4 ensures appropriate and effective oversight of Service Providers. Cash App must also periodically conduct a risk assessment to determine whether it offers or maintains Covered Accounts that would be subject to the Programʼs oversight. An adequate Program ensures that relevant Identity Theft Red Flags are detected, appropriately responded to, and periodic reviews are conducted such that changing risks and/or market conditions are appropriately accounted for. 2 2.2. Identification of Red Flags Where applicable, Cash App has opted to include the five categories noted below that the Federal Trade Commission (“FTCˮ)
documents.CP-020: Customer Conduct & Staff Protection Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Mar 25, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 25, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Customer Conduct & Staff Protection Policy (the "Policy") outlines Block, Inc.'s approach to persons whose behavior has been assessed as being abusive. The Consumer Protection Compliance Lead is responsible for management of this Policy. The purpose of this Policy is to establish comprehensive guidelines governing Block's approach to managing unacceptable customer behavior and ensuring consistent operations while maintaining brand-specific flexibility. Scope: This Policy is established by Block, Inc., its subsidiaries, and affiliates (collectively, "Block") and is applicable to all entities within Block. Definitions: Terminology Definition Abusive Behavior Abusive behavior refers to any behavior that is harmful, threatening, or offensive to another person or group. Aggressive Behavior Confrontational, forceful, or intimidating language that exceeds respectful communication boundaries. Examples include berating staff, hostile language, or refusing constructive dialogue. Discrimination Treating someone unfairly based on their race, gender, age, religion, sexual orientation, or other characteristic. Disrespectful Behavior Any behavior that lacks respect, such as mocking, belittling, or ignoring someone's boundaries. Harassment Unwanted or intimidating behavior that creates a confrontational environment. Examples include persistent belittling or demeaning of staff. 1 Immediate Threat Behavior posing direct risk to health, safety, or wellbeing of staff that requires urgent intervention. Persistent Contact Customers who engage in nuisance behavior, by exhibiting persistent contact attempts that are vexatious in nature. Physical Violence Physical aggression or force used against another person, such as hitting, pushing, or using weapons to cause harm. Reasonably Forceful An individual who asserts themselves firmly without resorting to aggressive behavior. This is considered acceptable behavior. Sexual Nature Content, language, or behavior related to sexuality or intimate relationships that is inappropriate for professional interaction. Staff Member All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Program, and include interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. Stalking Pattern of repeated unwanted attention, harassment, contact, or behavior that causes staff to feel threatened or unsafe. Threat Expressions of intent to cause harm or negative consequences, such as threatening to harm someone physically or damage their property. Unacceptable Behavior Conduct that deviates from established norms of civility, respect, and cooperation. Unacceptable Public Actions or behaviors that disrupt operations or threaten staff safety in Expression public spaces, including unauthorized recording or photographing of staff. Verbal Harassment Any kind of verbal abuse, such as name-calling, insulting, or making threats. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1. Policy Principles Block upholds the principle that everyone has the right to express their dissatisfaction, provide comments and offer suggestions to contribute to the enhancement of its services. Block also acknowledges that during challenging or distressing situations, people may display behavior inconsistent with their usual conduct when engaging with Block. It is important to note that Block does not categorize assertive or determined approaches as unacceptable or abusive behavior. 2 2.2. Unacceptable and Abusive Behavior Block Staff Members are entitled to a workplace free of abusive, offensive or threatening behavior even when a customer is under stress. Some examples of unacceptable behavior might include: ● Abusive or offensive language directed at Staff Members ● Remarks of a sexual nature directed at Staff Members ● Racist language direct

documents.CP-024: Loan Loss and Charge-Off Policy Cash App | US Last Approved: Mar 25, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 25, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: As a service provider in the financial industry, it is the policy of Cash App US (“Cash Appˮ) to develop, implement, and maintain this Loan Loss and Charge-Off Policy (“Policyˮ) for its Credit Products Borrow, Pre-Purchase Financing, Retroactive Financing, and Afterpay Pay Monthly). The purpose of this Policy is to describe the terms under which Cash App will charge-off accounts on its servicing platforms. Blockʼs Head of Underwriting & Credit and/or their designee is responsible for the management of this Policy. Cash App conducts business in compliance with applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Policy is to document Cash Appʼs loan charge-off process in accordance with the Federal Financial Institutions Examination Councilʼs (“FFIECˮ) Uniform Retail Credit Classification and Account Management Policy, which establishes standards for the classification and treatment of retail credit in financial institutions. Scope: This Policy governs Cash Appʼs Credit Products and has been created to ensure that Cash App and relevant Employees are aware and comply with the expectations of the Policy. Definitions: Terminology Definition Account A continuing relationship established by a person with a financial institution or creditor to obtain a product or service for personal, family, household, or business purposes, including an extension of credit. Charge-Off A determination that an uncollected Account balance is unlikely to be collected. The determination to recharacterize the uncollected Account balance as a charge-off does not discharge the amount of the debt owed by the Account holder. 1 Covered An Account that is primarily for personal, family, or household purposes. Account Credit Products Covered Accounts consisting of Cash App’s lending products (Borrow, Pre-Purchase Financing, Retroactive Financing, and Afterpay Pay Monthly) unless otherwise noted. Customer A consumer who is contractually obligated for repayment of an Account. Employee Staff members, contractors, temporary workers, and executive management. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1. Charge-Off Guidelines The following are guidelines for the timing and circumstances of charged-off Credit Products: ● Accounts originated on or after October 1, 2024 will be charged off no later than upon becoming 90 cumulative days delinquent from the contractual due date. Accounts originated before October 1, 2024 will be charged off no later than upon becoming 120 cumulative days delinquent from the contractual due date. ● Accounts in bankruptcy are charged off no later than 30 days from receipt of notification of the filing of bankruptcy, unless there is clear demonstration and document that repayment is likely to occur. ● Accounts that have been disputed by customers and identified as fraudulent are charged off no later than 30 days of discovery or when loss is determined, whichever is shorter. ● Accounts of deceased persons are charged off no later than 30 days from receipt of notification of the deceased status. Actual credit losses on individual Credit Products should be recorded when Cash App becomes aware of the loss, but in no case should the charge-off exceed the timeframes stated in this policy. A loss is defined as an asset, or portion thereof, that is considered uncollectible, and of such little value that continuance on the books is not warranted. A loss does not mean the asset has absolutely no recovery of salvage value; rather, it is not practical or desirable to defer writing off an essentially worthless asset, or portion thereof, even though partial recovery may occur in the future. When an Account is charged off, Cash App will place a note on the Account in the designated system of record, and Cash App will maintain an appropriate charge-off coding system to ensure a permanent record in its data systems. 2 2.2. documents.CP-030: Vulnerable Customer Standard Clearpay Last Approved: Mar 26, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 26, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The purpose of this Vulnerable Customer Standard (“Standardˮ) is for Clearpay Finance Limited (“Clearpayˮ) to establish a comprehensive framework for engaging with vulnerable customers when providing its regulated products and services. Clearpayʼs aim is to ensure that every customer who has a characteristic or characteristics of vulnerability is treated fairly, and, where possible, in accordance with their needs. This requirement is designed to ensure that vulnerable consumers experience outcomes equal to those of other consumers, and receive consistently good outcomes. This Standard is Clearpay's enterprise vulnerability standard and establishes the primary framework for identifying, categorising, and supporting vulnerable customers across all areas of the business. Whilst this Standard sets out Clearpay's comprehensive approach to vulnerability, Clearpay recognises that vulnerability considerations are relevant throughout the organisation. Accordingly, other Clearpay policies, standards and procedures will reference and incorporate considerations relating to customer vulnerability where appropriate to their scope and purpose. Examples of areas where vulnerability considerations are embedded include, but are not limited to: ● Product Design and Development – ensuring products are designed with vulnerable customers' needs in mind ● Marketing and Financial Promotions – ensuring communications are fair, clear, and not exploitative ● Credit Decisioning and Affordability Assessments – considering vulnerability factors in lending decisions ● Customer Communications – ensuring all communications are accessible and easy to understand ● Collections and Arrears Management – tailoring approaches to vulnerable customers' circumstances ● Complaints Handling – providing enhanced support to vulnerable customers during the complaints process ● Data Protection and Privacy – ensuring sensitive personal information is handled appropriately ● Third Party Management – ensuring outsourced service providers understand and comply with vulnerability requirements ● Staff Training and Competence – ensuring all relevant staff are equipped to identify and support vulnerable customers All Clearpay Employees are responsible for considering vulnerability in their day-to-day work and ensuring that customers are treated fairly, regardless of whether they have been formally identified as vulnerable. This enterprise-wide approach ensures that vulnerability is not treated as an isolated concern but is embedded within Clearpay's culture and business practices. Scope: This Standard applies to Clearpay, all its employees, outsourced service providers, consultants, contractors, suppliers and agents (“Clearpay Employeesˮ). It is the duty of all Clearpay Employees to be familiar with this Standard and any operational routines concerning vulnerable or potential vulnerable customers which are relevant to their work. Definitions: Terminology Definition FCA Financial Conduct Authority Vulnerable The FCA's definition: "someone who, due to their personal circumstances, is Customer especially susceptible to detriment, particularly when a firm is not acting with appropriate levels of care." Retail Customers Customers in the context of Clearpay’s regulated consumer credit agreements. PATHways Clearpay's model used by staff to manage in-depth conversations and Model categorise vulnerability. 2 2. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Square Capital, LLC, ("Square Capital" or "the Company") seeks to comply with all requirements that stem from regulations that govern commercial lending activities. documents.CP-039: Controlling the Assault of Non-Solicited Pornography and Marketing Act Policy Square | Square Capital, LLC | Credit Card Last Approved: October 3, 2025 Effective Date: October 3, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Controlling the Assault of Non-Solicited Pornography and Marketing Act ("CANSPAM") Policy ("Policy") outlines Square Capital, LLC's ("Square Capital" or the "Company") approach to compliance with the CANSPAM (or the "Act") and upholding Square Capital business requirements that do not run counter to the Act. This Policy was created and adopted with the sole focus on Square Capital's commercial credit card program and the Chief Compliance Officer oversees the development and implementation of this Policy document. This Policy establishes the framework by which Square Capital, the Initiator of email advertising communications, ensures that email advertising ("Commercial Email") complies with the requirements of CANSPAM. The purpose of this Policy is to ensure that Square Capital establishes and adheres to a consistent process for crafting email content and subject lines, sending of email advertising, and proper handling of opt-out requests. Background CANSPAM was enacted to prevent abuse in the use of Commercial Emails, and among other things, requires that Commercial Emails provide consumers with a means of opting-out of future solicitations. The Children's Online Privacy Protection Act ("COPPA") prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in connection with the collection, use, and/or disclosure of personal information from and about children, under the age of 13, on the Internet. COPPA provides privacy protections for children and specifically requires operators of websites and email directed to children to provide notice to and obtain consent from parents before collecting data from their children. The Federal Trade Commission ("FTC"), the nation's consumer protection agency, enforces COPPA, which spells out what operators of websites and online services must do to protect children's privacy and safety online. Both of these Acts are implemented by regulations issued by the FTC. While the FTC does not regulate financial institutions, the laws give enforcement authority to banking regulatory agencies. Scope: This Policy is established by Square Capital and is applicable to Square Capital's credit card product. All Square Capital business units, staff, and contractors, including those from vendors and third parties (i.e., Square Inc.), must comply with the aspects of this Policy. Square Capital recognizes a further duty to support the compliance programs of its Bank Partner, where applicable. Definitions: Terminology Definition Affirmative Consent With respect to a commercial electronic mail message ("email"), it means that: ● The recipient expressly consented to receive the message, either in response to a clear and conspicuous request for such consent or at the recipient's own initiative; and ● If the message is from a party other than the party to which the recipient communicated such consent, the recipient was given clear and conspicuous notice at the time the consent was communicated that the recipient's electronic mail address could be transferred to such other party for the purpose of initiating commercial electronic mail messages. Commercial Email Any email that has a primary purpose of commercial advertisement or promotion of a commercial product or service. Initiator Any person, including business entities and nonprofit associations, that initiates commercial e-mail messages must comply with the CANSPAM Act requirements if it either: ● Originates or transmits the e-mail; or ● Procures the transmission of the e-mail, meaning that the business either intentionally pays or provides other consideration to, or induces, another person to transmit the e-mail on its behalf. A person is not an Initiator when their role in initiating the Commercial Email is solely involved in routine conveyance.
documents.CP-040: Credit Card Privacy Policy Square | Square Capital, LLC | Credit Card Last Approved: October 3, 2025 Effective Date: October 3, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Credit Card Privacy Policy (“Policyˮ) outlines Square Capital, LLCʼs (“Square Capitalˮ or the “Companyʼ) approach to compliance with the Right to Financial Privacy Act (“RFPAˮ) and Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act (“GLBAˮ). The Chief Compliance Officer (“CCOˮ) oversees the development and implementation of this Policy and has delegated ownership of this Policy document to the Square Head of Compliance. Square Capital conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Policy is to further Square Capitalʼs compliance with RFPA and GLBA. Square Capital is committed to fully complying with RFPA and GLBA and to upholding Square Capital business requirements that do not run counter to the Act. Scope: This Policy is established by Square Capital and is applicable to Square Capitalʼs credit card product. It applies to all activities that involve non-affiliated third parties and the disclosure of nonpublic personal information NPI of customers for Square Capital's credit card product. Square Capital engages with non-affiliated third parties for the carrying out of financial transactions and for marketing of financial products. Such vendors may include, but are not limited to, vendors that verify customer identification information. The use of NPI for these reasons does not require that Square Capital obtain explicit prior consent from the customer. As of the enacting of this Policy, Square Capital may engage with any non-affiliated third party so that the non-affiliate can market Square Capital products, subject to Squareʼs standard privacy due diligence practices of third parties. Definitions: Terminology Definition Customer An individual who has a continuing relationship with Square Capital under which Square Capital provides one or more financial products or services. For example, because Square Capital services the loans, an applicant that receives a loan from Square Capital becomes a customer. Personal Data Information related to an identifiable person or household. Data is considered personal data if it can be used to identify a person, directly or indirectly. Nonpublic Personal The nonpublic portion of personal data, including any customer lists. Information (“NPIˮ) Nonpublic personal information consists of nonpublic information that is collected in connection with providing a financial product or service. Specifically, it means: ● Personally identifiable financial information, which includes: ○ Information a customer provides on an application for a loan or other financial product or service; ○ Account balance information, payment history, overdraft history and credit or debit card purchase information; ○ Information that a customer provides to Square Capital (or an agent thereof) that Square Capital obtains in connection with collecting on a loan or servicing a loan, as the case may be; ○ Any information collected through an Internet “cookieˮ; ○ Information from a customer report; ○ The fact that an individual is or has been a customer of Square Capital or has obtained a financial product or service from Square Capital; and ○ Any information about Square Capitalʼs customer if it is disclosed in a manner that indicates that the individual is or has been a Square Capital customer. ● Any list, description, or other grouping of customers that is derived using any personally identifiable financial information that is not publicly available. Lists include, but are not limited to, any list of individualsʼ names and addresses that is derived in whole or in part using personally identifiable information that is not publicly available, such as acc

Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1. Policy Statement Square Capital adheres to the requirements of ESign for all of its product offerings. Federal laws, regulations, and guidelines related to ESign are regulated by the Federal Trade Commission, providing lending Records and disclosures electronically. The Companyʼs goal is to implement procedures that: ● Facilitate e-commerce and make legally valid Electronic Transactions; ● Prevent deception and fraud in Transactions involving Electronic Records; and ● Ensure that customers can access Information Square Capital sends electronically. 2.2. documents.CP-042: Equal Credit Opportunity Act Policy Square | Square Capital, LLC | Credit Card Product Last Approved: October 3, 2025 Effective Date: October 3, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Equal Credit Opportunity Act (“ECOAˮ) Policy (“Policyˮ) outlines Square Capital, LLCʼs (“Square Capitalˮ or the “Companyˮ) approach to compliance with the Equal Credit Opportunity Act (“ECOAˮ or the “Actˮ) and to upholding requirements that do not run counter to the Act. The Policy was created and adopted with the sole focus on Square Capitalʼs commercial credit card program. The Chief Compliance Officer (“CCOˮ) oversees the development and implementation of this Policy and has delegated ownership of this Policy document to the Square Head of Compliance. Square Capital conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Policy is to outline Square Capitalʼs compliance with ECOA and to uphold Square Capital business requirements that do not run counter to the Act. Scope: This Policy is established by Square Capital and is applicable to Square Capitalʼs credit card product. All Square Capital business units, Staff Members, and contractors, including those from vendors and third parties (i.e., Square, Inc.) must comply with all aspects of this Policy. Square Capital recognizes a further duty to support the compliance program of its Bank Partner, where applicable. Definitions: Terminology Definition Adverse Action The term means: ● A refusal to grant credit in substantially the amount or on substantially the same terms as requested in an Application; ● A termination of an account or an unfavorable change in the terms of an account that does not affect all of substantially all of a class of Square Capitalʼs accounts; or ● A refusal to increase the amount of credit available to an applicant to who has made an Application for an increase. Application Means a request for an extension of credit for the type of credit offered. There are two types of Applications: ● Complete: An Application is complete once Identity Verification Service (“IDVˮ) screening is successfully passed and Square Capital has received all information needed to evaluate an Application, as defined by Square Capital and rendered a credit review decision. ● Incomplete: An Application is incomplete when Identity Verification Service (“IDVˮ) screening is successfully passed but Square Capital has not received all information needed to evaluate an Application, as defined by Square Capital, and has not rendered a credit decision. Inquiries and pre-qualification requests related to credit are not Applications. However, an inquiry or pre-qualification request becomes an Application if Square Capital evaluates information about a person, determines the person does not or does not qualify for a credit card or pre-qualification order, and communicates that decision to that person. This still applies whether the communication of a decision is intentional or inadvertent. Examples of responses that can turn an inquiry into an Application: ● “We can approve your Application for at least the minimum offer amount because you have high GPV and no chargebacks.ˮ ● Your credit score qualifies so go ahead and submit an Application and we will approve you.ˮ Creditor The person or entity that is intending to grant a credit extension. In the credit card program, the Creditor is Celtic Bank and Square Capital is considered the servicer to this program. Discrimination Means to treat an applicant less favorably than other applicants. Against an Applicant Prohibited Basis Means race, color, religion, national origin, sex, sexual identity or sexual orientation, marital status, or age (provided that the applicant has the capacity to enter into a binding contract); the fact that all or part of the applicantʼs income derives from any public assistance program; or the fact that the applicant has in good faith exercised any right under the Consumer Credit Protection Act
documents.CP-043: Fair and Accurate Credit Transactions Act Policy Square | Square Capital, LLC | Credit Card Last Approved: October 3, 2025 Effective Date: October 3, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Fair and Accurate Credit Transactions Act ("FCRA") Policy ("Policy") outlines Square Capital, LLC's ("Square Capital" or the "Company") approach to compliance with the FCRA as amended by the Fair and Accurate Credit Transactions Act ("FACTA"). The Chief Compliance Officer ("CCO") oversees development and implementation of this Policy and has delegated ownership of this Policy document to the Square Head of Compliance. The purpose of this Policy is to establish the responsibilities of Square Capital to comply with the requirements set forth in the FCRA, as amended. Scope: This Policy is established by Square Capital and is applicable to Square Capital. This Policy applies to Square Capital's credit card product, whether conducted by the Company's employees or through outsourced service providers. Square Capital recognizes a further duty to support the compliance programs of its Bank Partner, where applicable. Definitions: Terminology Definition Adverse Action A denial or revocation of credit, an unfavorable change in the terms of an existing credit arrangement, or a refusal to grant credit in substantially the amount or on substantially the terms that the applicant requested. It does not include an "accepted counteroffer," where the applicant requested particular credit card terms, such as a specific down payment or rate, but accepted the Company's offer for different terms, such as a higher down payment or rate. Affiliate Any Person or entity that is related by common ownership or common corporate control with another Person or entity. Consumer Any individual. Consumer Any written, oral, or other communication of any information by a Consumer Report Reporting Agency bearing on a Consumer's creditworthiness, credit standing, credit capacity, character, general reputation, Personal characteristics or mode of living. Consumer Generally refers to a credit bureau. If Square Capital shares information Reporting about Consumers with a third-party, the Company could become a Agency Consumer Reporting Agency. Covered Any account the Company offers or maintains that involves or is designed to Account permit multiple payments or transactions, and any other account the Company offers or maintains for which there is a reasonably foreseeable risk to Consumers or the safety and soundness of the Company from Identity Theft, including financial, operational, compliance, reputation or litigation risks. Creditor Any Person who regularly extends, renews, or continues credit; any Person who regularly arranges for the extension, renewal, or continuation of credit; or any assignee of an original Creditor who participates in the decision to extend, renew, or continue credit. Eligibility Includes any information that per the above definition would constitute a Information Consumer Report, plus the following: ● A report containing information solely as to transactions or experiences between the Consumer and the Person making the report; ● A communication of that information among Persons related by common ownership or Affiliated by corporate control; or ● A communication of other information among Persons related by common ownership or Affiliated by corporate control, if it is clearly and conspicuously disclosed to the Consumer that the information may be communicated among such Persons and the Consumer is given the opportunity, before the time that the information is initially communicated, to direct that such information not be communicated among such Persons. Eligibility information does not include aggregate or blind data that does not contain Personal identifiers such as account numbers, names, or addresses.
