documents.CP-044: Red Flags Policy Square | Square Capital, LLC | Credit Card Last Approved: October 3, 2025 Effective Date: October 3, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Red Flags Policy ("Policy") outlines Square Capital, LLC's ("Square Capital" or the "Company") approach to compliance with the Red Flags provision of the Fair and Accurate Credit Transactions Act ("FACTA"). The Chief Compliance Officer ("CCO") oversees the development and implementation of this Policy and has delegated ownership of this Policy to the Head of Business Product Compliance and Partnerships or Lending Product Compliance Lead as designee. This Policy is designed to effectively detect, prevent and mitigate Identity Theft pursuant to the rules and regulations established under FACTA known as the "Red Flag Rules." It applies to all customer accounts owned or maintained by Square Capital. Scope: This Policy is established by Square Capital and is applicable to Square Capital, specific to the credit card product. This Policy applies to Square Capital's lending products and associated activities, whether conducted by the Company's employees or through outsourced service providers, including when Square Capital acts as a service provider for a Bank Partner that originates lending products. Definitions: Terminology Definition Covered An account that a creditor offers or maintains for which there is a reasonably Account foreseeable risk to customers or to the safety and soundness of the creditor from Identity Theft, including financial, operational, compliance, reputation, or litigation risk. Identity Theft Fraud committed or attempted using one or more pieces of identifying information of another person without authority. Identifying information means any name or number that may be used, alone or in conjunction with any other information, to identify a specific person, including, but not limited to, any name, social security number, or date of birth. Red Flag A pattern, practice, or specific activity that indicates the possible existence of Identity Theft. Staff Member(s) All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Policy, and includes interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers and contractors. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1. Background Identity Theft is a problem for consumers who must contend with their identities being used to engage in unauthorized or fraudulent activities including illegitimate credit transactions and the negative impact that usage can render to a person's consumer credit score, and for creditors, who must absorb or otherwise write off transactions that result from stolen identities which can affect liquidity and material operations. Because Identity Theft is so closely linked to consumer credit reports, FACTA was passed as an amendment to the Fair Credit Reporting Act ("FCRA"). FACTA primarily addresses: ● Identity Theft Prevention and Credit History Restoration; ● Improvements in Use of and Consumer Access to Credit Information; ● Enhancing the Accuracy of Consumer Report Information; ● Limiting the Use and Sharing of Medical Information in the Financial System; ● Financial Literacy and Education Improvement; and ● Protecting Employee Misconduct Investigations. The regulatory guidance for Identity Theft prevention suggests using 26 specific criteria that are either linked to consumer credit reports or that are normally associated with credit applications. Triggering one or more of those criteria in a credit transaction, regardless of whether a consumer credit report is used or not, is a "Red Flag" that the identity being used in the transaction may be compromised which then requires the creditor to determine an appropriate course of action. This is why the term "Red Flag" is used to describe a creditor's program that seeks to prevent the usage of stolen identities. Usage of the 26 Red Flags is not required, but if not used it is expected that

Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Servicemembers Civil Relief Act ("SCRA") Policy ("Policy") outlines Square Capital, LLC's ("Square Capital" or the "Company") approach to compliance with the Servicemembers Civil Relief Act of 2003 (the "SCRA" or the "Act"). Child For purposes of identifying a dependent child, a person who is unmarried and: Who is under the age of eighteen years; Who, before attaining the age of eighteen years, became permanently incapable of self-support; or Who, after attaining the age of eighteen years and until completion of education or training (but not after attaining the age of twenty-three years), is pursuing a course of instruction at an approved educational institution; AND Who is a legitimate child, A legally adopted child, A stepChild who is a member of a veteran's household or was a member at the time of the veteran's death, Or an illegitimate Child but, as to the alleged father, only if acknowledged in writing signed by him, or if he has been judicially ordered to contribute to the child's support or has been, before his death, judicially decreed to be the father of such child, or if he is otherwise shown by evidence satisfactory to the Secretary to be the father of such child. Coverage Period The amount of time in days for the application of SCRA Benefits and protections. A Coverage Period may be one or more periods of time of no less than 30 consecutive days in length, and may be extended at any time. Interest Includes the money paid regularly at a particular rate for the use of money lent and service charges, renewal charges, fees or any other charges (except bona fide insurance) with respect to a credit obligation or liability. Military Service Is the primary requirement for the Benefits and protections, which is defined as (any): Active Duty, for members of the Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, or Coast Guard; In the case of a member of the National Guard, includes service under a call to active service authorized by the President or the Secretary of Defense for a period of more than 30 consecutive days under section 502(f) of title 32 for purposes of responding to a national emergency declared by the President and supported by Federal funds; Active service, in the case of a Servicemember who is a commissioned officer of the Public Health Service or the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration; or Any period during which a Servicemember is absent from duty on account of sickness, wounds, leave, or other lawful cause. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Policy communicates Square Capital, LLC's commitment to compliance with both consumer and commercial fairness requirements through: ● The Unfair, Deceptive, or Abusive Acts or Practices (“UDAAPˮ) Standards contained in sections 1031 and 1036 of the Dodd-Frank Act ● The Unfair or Deceptive Acts or Practices (“UDAPˮ) provisions in Section 5 of the Federal Trade Commission Act ● State laws prohibiting similar acts or practices in both consumer and commercial contexts This Policy sets forth comprehensive guidelines governing Square Capital, LLC, ensuring consistent operations while maintaining appropriate flexibility to address unique business needs. Square Capital's prevention of UDAAP and UDAP violations requires transparency, truthfulness, suitability, and fairness across all transactions and dealings with both consumers and merchants. The distinction between UDAAP and UDAP requirements reflects the different contexts in which Square Capital, LLC operates: ● Consumer financial products and services (subject to UDAAP under Dodd-Frank) ● Commercial and merchant services (subject to UDAP under the FTC Act) ● Products with potential dual consumer and commercial applications This comprehensive approach ensures appropriate protections while recognizing the different needs and sophistication levels of consumers and commercial customers. Scope: This Policy applies to Square Capital, LLC (collectively, "Square Capital"). This policy does not apply to Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Block, Inc.ʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation. Commercial A transaction primarily for business, commercial, or agricultural purposes, Transaction including merchant services and business lending products. Deceptive A representation, omission, act or practice that: 1 misleads or is likely to mislead the consumer or merchant; 2 the consumer's or merchant's interpretation is reasonable under the circumstances; and 3 the misleading representation, omission, act or practice is material. Material Information that is likely to affect a consumer's or merchant's choice of, or Information conduct regarding, a product or service. Staff Member(s) An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labor, contractors) hired by the Company who supports the operations covered by this Policy. Substantial Injury Financial or other harm that is more than trivial and may include monetary loss, unwarranted health and safety risks, and other forms of economic or social harm. Third-Party Any external entity that performs activities on behalf of Square Capital, Service Provider including vendors, contractors, and service providers who may interact with consumers or merchants. UDAAP Unfair, Deceptive, or Abusive Acts or Practices as prohibited by sections 1031 and 1036 of the Dodd-Frank Act, which apply to consumer financial products and services. UDAP Unfair or Deceptive Acts or Practices as prohibited by Section 5 of the Federal Trade Commission Act, which apply to both consumer and commercial transactions. 2 Unfair An act or prac

documents.CP-052: Acquiring Chargebacks Standard Cash App Last Approved: Aug 8, 2025 Effective Date: Aug 8, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Cash App's Acquiring Chargebacks Standard explains how Cash App processes Chargebacks received from our Customers' banks. When a Customer uses their credit or debit card for a transaction, they might later question or dispute that payment with their bank. This can happen for various reasons, such as: ● Unauthorized card use ● Unhappiness with a product or service ● Other transaction concerns The Chargeback Process Protocol outlines at a high level how this process works. 1. The cardholder initiates a dispute through their financial institution Issuing Bank) 2. Upon review, the Issuing Bank may initiate a formal chargeback procedure through the card network 3. The chargeback notification is transmitted to Cash App through established network channels 4. As the merchant of record, Cash App evaluates the chargeback and may: a. Conduct a transaction review b. Submit a response to validate the original transaction c. Accept Chargeback liability The Chargeback process adheres to the standardized protocols established by card network regulations to ensure proper dispute resolution. Scope: This document is relevant for Acquiring Chargebacks related to Cash App transactions conducted through debit or credit card, excluding Cash App Pay. Cash App is a line of business under Block, Inc., and is applicable to Cash App U.S. As of July 2025, true fraud evaluations (see 2.1.1 New Chargebacks) are not supported for AmEx or any payments still processed through the Cash Service Franklin. Addressing AmEx and migrating the remaining Franklin payments to new services is planned for Q3 '25. This means by Q4'25 all transaction types should be supported. Acquiring Chargebacks Standard ┃Cash App 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Acquirer An Acquiring or Merchant bank. A financial institution that provides services to Merchants that enable them to accept branded credit and debit cards for payment. Arbitration The final stage of the Chargeback lifecycle where the Network makes a binding decision to resolve a Chargeback when all prior resolution attempts have failed. Chargeback It is a financial mechanism in which a debit or credit card transaction is reversed by the cardholder's issuing bank. The Merchant who processed that transaction may accept the liability or represent the Chargeback with evidence of a valid transaction. Chargeback Cancellation This is the return of funds from a cardholder's issuing bank after a Chargeback was thought to be resolved as Merchant liability. Chargeback Reversed The Issuer accepts the Merchant Chargeback Representment as valid or otherwise does not continue the Chargeback; funds remain or are returned to Merchant; often referred to as "Won" by Merchant. Chargeback Upheld The Issuer continues the Chargeback process after receipt of the Merchant Chargeback Representment and maintains Merchant liability; generally referred to as "Lost" by Merchant. Clawback A term that refers to Cash App retrieving funds from a Consumer to cover the cost of a Chargeback that was received. documents.CP-055: Fraud Risk Management Standard Cash App | U.S. Last Approved: Jan 12, 2026 Effective Date: Jan 12, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Fraud Risk Management Standard ("Standard"), established by Cash App, U.S., a Line of Business under Block, Inc., outlines Cash App's strategy to detect, prevent, deter, and report on individuals who attempt to defraud Cash App Customers, gain access to Customer accounts, or move money using Cash App with fraudulent intent. The Consumer Protection Compliance Program Lead and/or their designee is responsible for management of this Standard. This Fraud Risk Management Standard is established by Compliance. References to "staff members" throughout this document shall refer to all employees or external workers (e.g., contingent labor, contractors) hired by Block, Inc. who supports the operations covered by this Standard. Cash App conducts business in compliance with applicable laws and regulations, maintaining and managing a combination of risk and compliance programs. The purpose of this Standard is to define the Fraud Risk Management Program and outline Cash App's strategy to ensure it upholds adequate risk and fraud mitigation strategies in line with industry expectations and relevant regulatory obligations. The activities in support of this Standard are administered cross-functionally and are designed to prevent and mitigate fraudulent activity on Cash App. Scope: This Standard is established by Cash App and is applicable to the Cash App business in the U.S. It defines the categories of fraudulent events, outlines the responsibilities of Staff to mitigate fraudulent activities and remove perpetrators of fraud from Cash App. Definitions: Terminology Definition Account Takeover ("ATO") When a third party maliciously gains access to a Consumer's account with the intention of using it for fraudulent purposes. Chargebacks When a cardholder files a dispute with their card issuing bank for a transaction funded by a linked debit or credit card. Consumer Consumer refers to an individual who is a natural person who obtains or has obtained a financial product or service that is to be used primarily for personal, family or household purposes. Customer Any individual, entity, or organization that has engaged in a transaction, purchased goods or services, or interacted with Block in a manner that establishes a direct relationship or affiliation. Denylisting The process by which Cash App accounts are closed after a determination or suspicion of involvement in harmful, fraudulent or illegal activity on Cash App. Fraud Any intentional act to obtain access to a Consumer's funds by deception, deceit, misrepresentation, or without authority. Fraudster An individual who commits an intentional act to gain access to or take Consumer funds without authority. Inauthentic Identities A label applied when Cash App has a reasonable level of knowledge that the underlying account holder is not the same as the identity that was used to successfully pass the identity verification process. Know Your Customer ("KYC") A legal requirement designed to prevent and monitor for identity theft, money laundering, terrorist financing, and other types of illegal activity by identifying a Consumer and understanding the purpose of the Consumer's account. Terminology Definition Machine Learning Model A machine learning model is a data transformation that has been ("MLM") trained to recognize certain types of behaviors. A model is trained on a set of data, providing it an algorithm that it can use to reason over and learn from. Fueled by data, machine learning models are the mathematical engines of artificial intelligence. Scam A dishonest scheme or activity designed to deceive individuals by persuading them to make an authorized transaction sent under false pretenses. Scammer A type of Fraudster that specializes in perpetrating Scams. Staff Members An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labor, contractors) hired by the

Complaints are more than just the ‘words’ but also are identified by the tone of and intention behind the Consumer outreach, as well as sentiments of a breach in the business’ responsibilities. Complaints may also include allegations that Cash App engaged in wrongful or unfair conduct. Complaints do not include disputes of authorized or erroneous electronic funds transfers, which are handled through a distinct process according to relevant policies and procedures. Consumer Consumer refers to an individual who is a natural person who obtains or has obtained a financial product or service from Cash App that is to be used primarily for personal, family or household purposes. Consumer Harm Consumer Harm is any actual or potential loss or injury to a Consumer, whether financial or non-financial, that may be caused by the violation of federal or state consumer protection laws or regulations, or by weaknesses in the compliance management system. Credit Products Credit Products encompass Cash App US’s lending products (Cash App Borrow, Pre-Purchase Financing, and Retroactive Financing) unless otherwise noted. Fair Lending The unbiased treatment of all Consumers when making credit-related decisions and the availability of credit to all creditworthy applicants without regard to race, color, religion, national origin, sex, marital status, or age (provided the applicant has the capacity to contract); to the fact that all or part of the applicant's income derives from a public assistance program; or to the fact that the applicant has in good faith exercised any right under the Consumer Credit Protection Act. Fair Lending also prohibits creditor practices that discriminate on the basis of any of these factors. Legal Tag A classification used to identify and categorize Complaints that involve potential legal issues or have legal implications. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Customer Communication Standard (“Standardˮ) outlines Squareup International Limitedʼs (“SILˮ) approach for designing, testing, and monitoring customer communications. The Head of Compliance is responsible for management of this Standard. The purpose of this Standard is to ensure that SIL communicates with customers in a manner that is clear, fair, and not misleading, supporting good outcomes and consumer understanding while meeting applicable laws and regulatory expectations. Scope: This Standard applies to all oral and written customer communications across SILʼs channels, including but not limited to: ● Website and app content ● Onboarding and welcome communications ● Account updates ● Marketing and promotional materials ● Product disclosures (e.g., terms, pricing, and fees) ● Sales and Customer support interactions ● Social media/Telephone communications ● Complaint responses This Standard does not apply to: ● Internal employee communications or training materials ● Communications related to supplier or partner agreements ● Automated transaction confirmations (e.g., receipts) where no decision-making is involved. It covers both new and existing communications, ensuring legacy content is also brought up to Standard. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Staff Member(s) An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labor, contractors) hired by the Company who supports the operations covered by this Standard. documents.CP-059: Hardship Support Standard Square | Square AU Pty Ltd Last Approved: Feb 9, 2026 Effective Date: Feb 9, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: At Square AU Pty Ltd, we understand that customers may experience financial hardship due to unforeseen events or changing circumstances. As a responsible financial services provider, we are committed to supporting customers during difficult times with empathy, fairness, and transparency. Our approach is guided by legal and regulatory obligations, including the Corporations Act 2001, Australian Consumer Law ACL and relevant ASIC guidance on hardship support, complaints handling, and fair treatment. We offer timely, flexible, and practical assistance tailored to individual needs, with clear and accessible communication channels. We also recognise that financial hardship can intersect with broader forms of vulnerability, and we are committed to providing support that is inclusive, respectful, and responsive. This policy outlines our commitment to helping customers in financial difficulty and ensuring they are treated with care and dignity. Scope: This Standard applies to all Square AU employees, contractors, and representatives who interact with customers, including but not limited to: ● Square sellers (merchants using Square products and services) ● Individuals receiving customer service, onboarding assistance, or technical support ● Individuals lodging complaints, disputes, or requests for help ● Business owners or operators disclosing business or personal hardships The Standard applies across all products and services offered by Square AU, including Square Loans, payments, point-of-sale, online commerce, business software, and associated support functions. Definitions: Terminology Definition Financial A situation where a customer is willing but temporarily unable to meet Hardship financial obligations due to events such as illness, job loss, natural disaster, or family violence. Vulnerable A customer who, due to personal circumstances, requires special care or Customer support, such as individuals with cognitive impairment, mental illness, or language barriers. 2. Square AUSpecific Requirements 2.1. Understanding Financial Hardship Financial hardship refers to a situation where a customer is willing but temporarily unable to meet their financial obligations due to unforeseen circumstances. These circumstances may include, but are not limited to, loss of employment or reduced income, serious illness or injury, domestic or family violence, the impact of natural disasters, a relationship breakdown, increased cost of living pressures, or the death of a family member. Square AU acknowledges that financial hardship can be short-term or long-term in nature and may affect individuals differently depending on their personal and financial situation. We are committed to assessing each case with sensitivity and fairness. 2.2. Regulatory and Legal Context As an Australian Financial Services Licence AFSL holder and payment services provider, Square AU is subject to several key obligations: ● Corporations Act 2001 (s912A): We must act efficiently, honestly, and fairly, maintain adequate resources, and implement compliant dispute resolution procedures. ASIC expects licensees to proactively support customers in hardship, including tracking hardship complaints and ensuring systems and staff are equipped to respond. ● ASIC Expectations RG 271 and Report 782 ASIC expects accessible hardship channels, tailored responses based on individual circumstances, appropriate staff training, additional support for vulnerable customers, and internal tracking of hardship data. ● Australian Consumer Law ACL We must avoid unconscionable or misleading conduct. This includes treating hardship customers fairly and ensuring any representations made about support options are accurate. ● Australian Financial Complaints Authority AFCA As a member of AFCA, we are required to handle hardship disputes fairly, avoi
documents.CP-060: Vulnerable Customers Standard Square | Square AU Pty Ltd Last Approved: Feb 4, 2026 Effective Date: Feb 4, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Square AU is committed to treating all customers fairly, with empathy, and in line with its legal and ethical obligations. We recognise that vulnerability or hardship can affect a personʼs ability to access services, understand information or manage their affairs. Our approach is guided by key regulatory frameworks, including: ● Australian Consumer Law, which prohibits unconscionable conduct and promotes fair treatment, especially for vulnerable consumers. ● ASIC RG 271, which sets expectations for handling complaints from vulnerable and disadvantaged individuals. ● ASIC Report 782, which highlights the importance of proactive support for small businesses in hardship. We also uphold federal anti-discrimination laws, including the Age, Disability, Sex, and Racial Discrimination Acts, and the Australian Human Rights Commission Act, to ensure inclusive, respectful service. Vulnerability can arise beyond traditional lending contexts. Many Square sellers are small businesses facing external pressures, and while Square AU may not cause that vulnerability, we recognise our role in supporting these customers with flexible and compassionate solutions. This Standard sets out how we identify and assist vulnerable customers to meet regulatory expectations and reflect our values of fairness, accessibility, and economic empowerment. Scope: This Standard applies to all Square AU employees, contractors, and representatives who interact with customers, including but not limited to: ● Square sellers (merchants using Square products and services) ● Individuals receiving customer service, onboarding assistance, or technical support ● Individuals lodging complaints, disputes, or requests for help ● Business owners or operators disclosing business or personal hardship The Standard applies across all products and services offered by Square AU, including payments, point-of-sale, online commerce, business software, and associated support functions. Definitions: Terminology Definition Vulnerability A state which, due to personal circumstances or characteristics, an individual is especially susceptible to detriment or harm, particularly when a firm is not acting with appropriate levels of care. Vulnerability can be temporary, recurring, or permanent. Vulnerable An individual who, due to their personal circumstances, is especially Customer susceptible to harm or disadvantage, particularly if Square AU fails to act with appropriate levels of care. This includes customers who may have difficulty accessing services, understanding information, making decisions, or managing their financial affairs effectively. Hardship A situation where a customer is willing but unable to meet their financial obligations due to illness, unemployment, or other reasonable cause. This includes both personal and business-related financial difficulties. 2. Square AU Specific Requirements Square AU recognises that customer vulnerability can affect a person's ability to effectively engage with our products, services, or support channels. In line with our obligations under Australian Consumer Law, we are committed to avoiding conduct that is misleading, deceptive, or unconscionable, particularly where a customer is at a disadvantage. Additionally, under ASIC Regulatory Guide 271 RG 271, we are expected to identify and appropriately support vulnerable customers—especially throughout the complaints process. This Standard outlines the minimum expectations for recognising and responding to customer vulnerability across all interactions, including in hardship, complaint handling, and everyday service delivery, to ensure fair and inclusive outcomes. 2.1. Definition of a Vulnerable Customer A vulnerable customer is an individual who, due to personal circumstances or characteristics, is at increased risk of detriment, harm, or disadvantage,
documents.EE-002: Global Modern Slavery Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: October 3, 2025 Effective Date: October 3, 2025 1. Overview At Block, we are committed to supporting the freedom, dignity, and well-being of all people connected to our ecosystem. Respect for human rights is at the center of our culture and operations. This commitment means we take action to foster equitable working places for our employees and business partners, and eradicate the existence of involuntary labor and exploitation in our business and extended supply chain. Block strictly prohibits all forms of modern slavery in our operations. Our supply chain may include business in jurisdictions or industries known to present a risk of human and labor exploitation. Block must take proactive steps to ensure our operations do not cause, contribute to, or have links to exploitation and modern slavery. Modern slavery affects millions of victims worldwide and takes many forms across industries. Means of compulsion can be violent and visible; they can also be subtle, as in the cases of debt bondage, extortion, or seizure of travel documents. Below are the most common forms of Modern Slavery covered by this Policy: - Forced labor is any work or service extracted from a person without consent and under threat of penalty. This includes some forms of state-imposed labor and criminal servitude that involve punishment on the basis of political, social, religious, or ethnic identity. - Child labor includes all uses, purchasing, or offering services of children for illicit or illegal exploitation, both commercial and private. - Debt bondage is the use of debt to secure labour or services below the fair value of that labour or where debt cannot be repaid. Debt bondage commonly takes the form of steep recruitment fees or transportation fees levied by employers that are never repaid. - Sexual exploitation includes the use, procurement, or offering of forced sexual labor as part of commercial exploitation, including all forms of commercial sexual exploitation of children. - Domestic servitude forces victims to reside inside of private homes, often without pay, and are subject to compelled labor as well as limitations on mobility and communication. - Human trafficking involves the recruitment, transportation, or harboring of individuals through coercive means, and also falls under modern slavery for the purpose of exploitation and profit. Our commitment to combat modern slavery aligns with various international conventions, principles, and standards, including the United Nationsʼ Universal Declaration of Human Rights, its Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights (“GPBHRˮ), and the International Labour Organizationʼs (“ILOˮ) core labor standards. These sources uphold the rights to life, liberty, security, fair treatment at work, and direct all companies to eliminate any vestiges of forced labor, child labor, and exploitation in respect of employment and occupation. Scope: This Policy applies to Block, Inc., its subsidiaries, and affiliates (collectively, “Blockˮ), including all Reporting Subsidiaries. Definitions: Terminology Definition Block Supplier Any third party involved in producing or delivering a product or service to Block, from raw materials to end consumers. Block Suppliers include direct and indirect suppliers, contractors, contingent workers, vendors, consulting firms, staffing agencies, and licensees. Staff Member(s) An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labor, contractors) hired by the Company who supports the operations covered by this Policy. Reporting Block subsidiaries that are subject to Modern Slavery Statement consultation Subsidiary and reporting requirements under local laws, including Australiaʼs Modern Slavery Act 2018, the U.K. Modern Slavery Act 2015, and Canadaʼs Fighting Against Forced Labour and Child Labour in Supply Chains Act, SC 2023. Criteria for Reporting Subsidiaries are specifically set forth under local statute, and Officers and Direct

Document Details 13 Global Anti-Bribery & Anti-Corruption Policy | Block 2 Confidential and Not for Distribution Definitions Terminology Definition Bribery or Bribe Anything of value offered, given, or received, including any financial or other advantage, in order to improperly influence a decision, action, or exercise of discretion by a Government Official or commercial party. Corruption Dishonest or fraudulent conduct, including the misuse or abuse of one’s power, influence, or position for personal benefit. Some common types include kickbacks, bribery, influence peddling, nepotism, handling the proceeds of a crime, bid rigging, and extortion. Facilitation Anything of value, typically a small payment or gift, offered or provided to Payment a Government Official to facilitate or expedite routine clerical or administrative, non‐ discretionary actions such as inspecting goods in transit, issuing permits and/or licenses, processing visas or providing police protection. Government Also known as Public Official, a Government Official is any elected or Official appointed officer, candidate for political office, employee, or other person performing service on behalf of a government, government agency, state‐owned or ‐controlled entity, military, political party, or public international organization (e.g. United Nations). This definition includes both U.S. and non‐U.S. Government Officials. Family members of government officials and individuals working at the express or implied direction of Government Officials, such as contractors, should be treated as Government Officials. Staff Includes all Block full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers, including interns, consultants, vendors, third party providers, and contractors. Global Anti-Bribery & Anti-Corruption Policy | Block 3 Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. Introduction This Anti-Bribery & Anti-Corruption Policy (“Policy”) outlines Block, Inc.’s (“Block”) approach to combating Bribery and Corruption risks within its business. Block’s Anti-Bribery and Corruption Program Lead is responsible for the management of this Policy. 2. Purpose Block’s mission requires it to act ethically and transparently everywhere it operates. Block does not tolerate bribery or corruption of any sort. Block, including members of its Board of Directors, officers, and staff, must maintain the highest ethical standards of business conduct and comply with all applicable anti-corruption and anti-bribery laws and regulations, including the U.S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (“FCPA”) and the U.K. Bribery Act, as well as the anti-corruption and anti-bribery laws of the countries where Block is doing business. Block’s global policy with respect to bribery and corruption is based on four key principles: ● Do not offer, give, or accept bribes or improper payments, gratuities, or remuneration in any form; ● Be ethical and transparent in interactions with Government Officials and third parties, including pre-clearance of any gifts or entertainment with Government Officials at go/disclose or ethics@; ● Speak up. documents.EE-004: Global Gifts and Entertainment Policy Block Inc. Last Approved: Mar 25, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 25, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The Global Gifts and Entertainment Policy (the “Policyˮ) covers Block Inc.ʼs requirements for managing business exchanges with Third Parties. Appropriate exchanges, such as gifts, meals, or entertainment, can help build and maintain relationships. However, when excessive, they may create unnecessary risk or conflicts for the organization. This Policy helps Staff Members avoid risks such as corruption, bribery, influence peddling, conflicts of interest, and kickbacks. Block is committed to maintaining the highest standards of business and personal ethics, as set forth in the Block Code of Conduct. References to “Staff Memberˮ throughout this document include all full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers, including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, Third Party providers, and contractors. This Policy is designed to help prevent conflicts of interest, undue influence, and situations that may be perceived as corruption. Complying with this Policy protects Blockʼs reputation for conducting business with integrity and safeguards Staff Members and their personal integrity. Scope: This Policy applies to Block Inc. (“Blockˮ) and its subsidiaries globally. It extends to Block officers, directors, temporary workers, third parties representing Block, and Block Staff Members. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Global Conflict of Interest Policy (“Policyˮ) is designed to help Block employees avoid even the appearance of a Conflict of Interest and recognize when to get help or report a concern. Scope: This Policy applies to Block and extends to employees of Block. Employees who have questions about a situation not covered in this Policy should contact Compliance at ethics@. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Block, Inc. and its subsidiaries (collectively, “Blockˮ) are committed to conducting business with honesty and integrity. This Global Speak Up Policy (“Policyˮ) sets out how Staff can speak up and express their concerns without fear of retaliation. documents.EE-008: Squareup Europe Limited Conflicts of Interest Policy Last Approved: Sep 29, 2025 Effective Date: Sep 29, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Squareup Europe Limited (“SELˮ) is an authorised Electronic Money Institution, regulated and supervised by the Financial Conduct Authority (“FCAˮ) under Firm Reference Number 900846. The indirect and ultimate parent company is Block, Inc., a Delaware corporation, and a merchant services aggregator and mobile payments company based in the United States of America. This Conflicts of Interest Policy (“Policyˮ) outlines SELʼs approach to identifying, managing, and mitigating conflicts of interest within the firm. SEL is dedicated to adhering to the principles of fair treatment and good outcomes for Customers, conducting itself with honesty, fairness, and professionalism, and acting in the best interests of its Customers. An important element of these principles is that SEL has appropriate arrangements in place to prevent Conflicts of Interest from adversely affecting the interests of SELʼs Customers. This Policy provides a clear statement of the standards around identifying, documenting, escalating and managing or preventing Conflicts of Interest. The objective of this Policy is to communicate SELʼs requirements regarding Conflicts of Interest and to advise all Staff Members of their obligation to disclose Conflicts of Interests and maintain high ethical standards. SEL and its Staff Members must maintain the highest possible standards of integrity in business relationships, both inside and outside the organization. Staff Members must: ● Reject any business practice which might be deemed improper (including practices that might benefit SEL where there is a Conflict of Interest); ● Never use authority or position for personal gain; ● Act at all times with impartiality, independence, and integrity; and ● Avoid being, or giving the appearance of being, in a position which may result in actual or perceived detriment to SEL's reputation and/or interests. Staff Members must take all reasonable and appropriate steps to prevent or avoid situations where a Conflict of Interest may happen, potentially arise, or have been perceived to have happened. It is not enough to rely solely on the disclosure and recording of (actual, potential or perceived) conflicts. Proactive steps must be taken to prevent Conflicts of Interest from occurring in the first place and to ensure that personal interests do not conflict with the wider interests of SEL or its Customers at any time. Staff are personally accountable for disclosing Conflicts of Interest and/or seeking direction on any issues which could potentially conflict with Staff responsibilities to SEL as they arise in line with the Policy Requirements Section 3 of this Policy. Where Conflicts of Interest are not properly managed, there may be serious consequences for SEL and for all those involved. In extreme cases, Conflicts of Interest may facilitate fraud, corruption, or other serious issues. Furthermore, they have the potential to negatively impact the business and tarnish the reputation of SEL. The person responsible could be subject to disciplinary action internally with SEL, or criminal sanctions externally (e.g. if it is deemed to be bribery or corruption). Scope: This Policy is established by SEL and is applicable to all SEL Staff Members. SEL requires all Staff Members to adhere to the standards set out in this Policy and any other SEL and Block Inc., standards which seek to prevent and manage Conflicts of Interest. Failure to comply with this Policy may result in disciplinary and/or legal action. As a member of the Block Inc. Group, SEL is subject to the Block, Inc. Global Code of Business Conduct and Ethics (“the Codeˮ) and Global Conflicts of Interest Policy and should read this Policy in connection with the Code and Global Conflicts of interest. If there is any conflict between the Code and applicable local law, the most restrictive requirement

documents.EE-010: Conduct Standards Policy Square | Squareup International Limited Last Approved: Mar 30, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 30, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Squareup International Limited (“SILˮ or “the Companyˮ) is authorised by the Central Bank of Ireland (the “CBI ˮ) as an e-money institution (“EMoney Firmˮ) pursuant to Regulation 9 of the European Communities Electronic Money) Regulations 2011 (as amended) (the “EMoney Regulationsˮ). The Company was incorporated on 26 May 2016 under registration number 583287 and was authorised as an EMoney Firm by the CBI on 18 June 2020 (with CBI reference number: C184117. The Central Bank Individual Accountability Framework) Act 2023 (the “Actˮ) provides for the introduction of the Individual Accountability Framework (“IAFˮ), which is designed to: ● Improve governance, performance, and accountability in regulated firms by establishing a framework of enhanced clarity as to who is responsible for what within these firms; ● Clarify the standards to be met by individuals holding these responsibilities; ● Promote the embedding of a culture of ethical compliance in regulated firms; and ● Achieve better outcomes for consumers and users of financial services and for the ongoing functioning of the economy. The four key areas covered by the Act are noted below and it is the first area, Conduct Standards which is governed by this Policy: ● Conduct Standards, which set out the standards of behaviour the CBI expects of firms and certain individuals working within them; ● A Senior Executive Accountability Regime (otherwise known as “SEARˮ) which ensures clearer accountability by imposing obligations on in-scope firms and senior managers within them to set out clearly where responsibility and decision-making lies for their business. Currently this requirement does not apply to SIL; ● Enhancements to the Fitness & Probity F&P Regime; and 1 ● Enhancements to CBIʼs Administrative Sanctions Procedure, including a key change regarding the CBIʼs ability to take enforcement directly against individuals for breaches of their obligations rather than only for their participation in breaches committed by a firm. The Conduct Standards, as a key element of the IAF and guidance on the IAF (the “Guidanceˮ) came into force in December 2023. As a regulated EMoney Firm, the Company and individuals performing certain roles in and on behalf of SIL, are subject to the Conduct Standards, and related provisions in the Act and the Guidance. The purpose of this Conduct Standard Policy (“Policyˮ) is to facilitate the Companyʼs compliance with these obligations. This Policy sets out the Conduct Standards that are applicable to SIL and its Controlled Functions CFs and Pre-Approval Controlled Functions PCFs and the related obligations under the Act and the Guidance. This Policy is an integral part of SILʼs internal IAF, and promotes the integration of the Conduct Standards into the culture and conduct of the affairs of SIL, in order to drive the right behaviours and standards in SIL. This Policy should be read in conjunction with the Companyʼs Conduct Standards procedure, and Fitness and Probity policy and procedures. The Companyʼs HR Business Partner can be contacted to obtain a copy of any of these documents, if needed. Scope: This Policy is established by SIL and is applicable to individuals that hold CFs or PCFs within SIL and individuals that perform CF or PCF roles on behalf of SIL. documents.EE-012: Fitness and Probity Policy Square | Squareup International Limited Last Approved: Mar 24, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 24, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Squareup International Limited (“SILˮ or referred to as “the Companyˮ) is authorised by the Central Bank of Ireland (the “CBI ˮ) as an e-money institution (“EMoney Firmˮ) pursuant to Regulation 9 of the European Communities Electronic Money) Regulations 2011 (as amended) (the “EMoney Regulationsˮ). The Company was incorporated on 26 May 2016 under registration number 583287 and was authorised as an EMoney Firm by the CBI on 18 June 2020 (with CBI reference number: C184117. In 2010, the Central Bank Reform Act 2010 (as amended) (the “Actˮ) created a new unified structure for financial regulation within Ireland and established the CBI as a single fully integrated structure with a unitary board – the CBI Commission. The Act enhances the system of regulatory control and confers additional powers on the CBI and its Governor to support the stability of the financial system and to protect users of financial services. As part of this, the Act harmonised a statutory system for the regulation of persons performing CFs or PCFs in regulated financial service providers. This allowed the CBI to introduce their F & P Regime. This regime has been enhanced by the Central Bank Individual Accountability Framework) Act 2023 (the “IAFˮ). The core aim of the F & P Regime is to ensure that persons in senior positions within firms are competent and capable, honest, ethical and of integrity, and also financially sound. As a regulated EMoney Firm, the Company is subject to the F & P Regime and the provisions of the Act. The purpose of this Policy is to facilitate the Companyʼs compliance with its obligations under the Act. This Policy also provides an explanation of the F & P Regime and the relevant requirements the Company must adhere to, in order to ensure CFs and PCFs are competent and capable, honest, ethical, of integrity and financially sound. 1 This Policy should be read in conjunction with the Companyʼs Fitness and Probity procedures and F & P Framework. The Companyʼs HR can be contacted to obtain a copy of any of these documents, if needed. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Speak Up Policy ("Policy") outlines SIL's approach to Protected Disclosures. Staff are to adhere to this Policy along with Block, Inc.'s ("Block") Global Code of Business Conduct and Ethics (the "Code") if they become aware of information which suggests that wrongdoing (as described in Section 2.1 may have happened or may happen if steps are not taken to address it. The EU Chief Compliance Officer ("CCO") is responsible for the management of this Policy. SIL conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Policy is to further SIL's commitment to maintaining the highest standards of honesty, openness and accountability and recognises that all Staff have an important role to play in achieving this purpose. SIL strives to conduct its business honestly and with integrity at all times. SIL culture is to encourage Staff to raise concerns of wrongdoing without fear of retaliation. This Policy and the Code guide Staff on how to raise any matters of genuine concern. SIL will not tolerate retaliation against or penalisation of anyone who uses this Policy correctly to raise concerns. Scope: This Policy is established by SIL and applies to all Staff, who acquire information in respect of a relevant wrongdoing (as described in Section 2.1 in a work-related context. Definitions Term Definition Staff All Squareup International Limited (“SILˮ) workers including current and former employees, officers, directors, consultants, independent contractors, trainees, interns, work experience trainees, part-time and fixed-term workers, casual and agency Staff, volunteers and executive and non-executive directors of the board of SIL. This includes those who are in a recruitment or other pre-contractual negotiation with SIL. 1 Term Definition Protected A Protected Disclosure is a disclosure made by Staff of relevant Disclosure information in accordance with law or this Policy, which in the Staff's reasonable belief, tends to show one or more relevant wrongdoings. Designated Person The EU Chief Compliance Officer is the Designated Person for the purposes of this Policy and, as such, is responsible for responding to reports of matters or concerns under this Policy. Reporter A Reporter is a member of Staff that reports matters or concerns under this Policy via internal reporting channels. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1 Protected Disclosures Protected Disclosures involve the disclosure of relevant information, which in the Staff's reasonable belief, tends to show one or more relevant wrongdoings. A Relevant Wrongdoing in relation to the workplace means that: ● An offence has been committed or is being or is likely to be committed; ● A person has failed, is failing or is likely to fail to comply with any legal obligation (other than an obligation arising under Staff's contract of employment or contract of services); ● A miscarriage of justice has occurred, is occurring or is likely to occur; ● The health and safety of a person has been, is or is likely to be endangered; ● The environment has been, is or is likely to be damaged; ● An unlawful or improper use of funds or resources of a public body or other public money has occurred or is likely to occur; ● A "Breach" has occurred - for the purposes of this Policy, a breach means a breach of the laws governing the following matters (the below is an non-exhaustive list): ○ financial services, products and markets, and prevention of money laundering and terrorist financing; ○ protection of privacy and personal data, and security of network and information systems; and ○ consumer protection. ● A Relevant Wrongdoing also includes information tending to show that any of the above matters has been, is being or is likely to be concealed or destroyed or an attempt has been, is being or is likely to be made to conceal or dest
documents.EE-014: Conflicts of Interest Policy Square Australia Last Approved: Dec 18, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 18, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Conflicts of Interest Policy ("Policy") sets out Square AU's approach to identifying, managing, and monitoring conflicts of interest, reflecting our commitment to strong corporate governance and ethical business conduct. Operating in a regulated environment, effective conflict management is essential to maintaining trust and integrity across all our activities. Under the Corporations Act 2001, Square AU is required to have arrangements in place to manage conflicts arising in connection with the provision of financial services. This Policy provides a clear framework to address potential, actual, and perceived conflicts, outlining the responsibilities of all representatives to adhere to these guidelines and promptly report any conflicts. The Chief Compliance Officer (“CCOˮ) has primary responsibility for overseeing and implementing this Policy. Through these measures, Square AU seeks to protect the interests of customers, employees, and stakeholders, ensure regulatory compliance, and promote an ethical culture throughout the organisation. The purpose of this Conflicts of Interest Policy is to provide a clear and structured framework for identifying, assessing, escalating, and managing conflicts of interest within Square AU. It ensures that all conflicts are addressed in a way that upholds Square AUʼs integrity and ethical standards, meets regulatory requirements, and safeguards the interests of customers, employees, and stakeholders. This Policy forms an integral part of Square AUʼs broader compliance and risk management framework, demonstrating our commitment to the highest standards of professional conduct and corporate responsibility. Scope: This Conflicts of Interest Policy is established by Square AU Pty Ltd ("Square AU") and is applicable to all employees, officers, directors, contractors, interns, contingent workers and representatives of Square AU. The Policy sets out the approach to identifying, managing, and mitigating conflicts of interest within the context of Square AU's operations. This Policy applies specifically to the activities and responsibilities of Square AU and its subsidiaries directly involved in the provision of financial services. It governs the conduct of all employees and representatives in relation to their professional duties and external engagements that may give rise to conflicts of interest. This document must be read in conjunction with Block Inc Policies and programs. Square AU will make reasonable attempts to align with Block Inc Policies unless regulatory obligations differ. Definitions: Terminology Definition Conflict of Interest ● An actual Conflict Of Interest (i.e. a Conflict Of Interest that has arisen); ● A potential Conflict Of Interest (i.e. a Conflict Of Interest that may arise given particular facts and circumstances); ● A perceived Conflict Of Interest (i.e. a situation which may give rise to the perception of a Conflict Of Interest); and ● Where a direct Conflict Of Interest may not in fact exist Actual Conflict of Interest A Conflict of Interest that is currently happening. For example, if an advisor recommends a product that benefits them financially, even though it may not be in the best interest of the client. Potential Conflict of Interest A situation where a Conflict of Interest may arise in the future. For example, an advisor who could benefit from the sale of a particular financial product in the future, though they haven't recommended it yet. Perceived Conflict of Interest A situation where it appears to others that a Conflict of Interest exists, even if there is no actual conflict. This is often based on appearances and can erode trust, even if the advisor is acting impartially. Managing Conflicts of Interest The process by which financial services licensees identify, assess, and address conflicts of interest to ensure that the
documents.EE-015: Communications Requirements Standard Cash App | Cash App Investing Last Approved: August 22, 2025 Effective Date: August 22, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Cash App Investing Communications Requirements Standard ("Standard") outlines Cash App Investing's approach to acceptable communications practices for Block employees. Cash App Investing conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Standard is to outline Cash App Investing's communications requirements. The Chief Compliance Officer for Cash App Investing, or their designee, is responsible for management of this Standard. Note: For any inquiries regarding what may be considered investing-related, Cash App Investing Compliance can be contacted by email (comms@cashappinvest.com) or Slack at #comms-policy. Scope: This Standard is established by Cash App Investing and is applicable to Block employees. Definitions: Terminology Definition Investing-related All communications relating to Cash App Investing LLC's ("CAI" or communication "Cash App Investing") stocks and exchange-traded funds ("ETFs") services are considered "investing-related". Examples include communications regarding troubleshooting a stock-related bug, discussing investing advertising and designing an investing screen for the app. Conversely, communications that are not related to CAI or CAI's services are excluded. Examples include human resources queries, information technology support requests, anything 100% bitcoin related, Employee Stock Purchase Plan questions, Jack Dorsey's "Ask Me Anything" channel, or other communications unrelated to CAI services. Employees Employees refer to all Block employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by these Standard, and include interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. 2. Program Requirements 2.1 Approved and Forbidden Platforms 2.1.1 Instant Messaging Employees are only permitted to use a Slack Investing workspace account to communicate, or must communicate in a direct message ("DM") or channel that is connected to the Slack Investing workspace. The use of Cash App or other Block workspaces (e.g. Square, Block, Tidal) is strictly prohibited unless the communication occurs in a DM or channel where at least one Slack Investing account is connected. For more information, please refer to the How to be Compliant with Slack section below. Text messaging, iMessages, Google Chat, Google Meet Chat, Discord, Signal, Messenger, WeChat, WhatsApp, QQ, Youni, ICQ, Skype, Signal, Wickr, Confide, and any other messaging platform are prohibited. Employees may use a prohibited platform to direct the recipient to an approved platform but must not include any details that are investing-related in the message. For example, a Slack message may be sent that says: "I need to discuss something with you. I have invited you to a channel on another workspace, please check your email for the invite." 2.1.2 Slack Employees who regularly communicate about Investing are required to have a Slack Investing account. There are several options for Employees who do not regularly communicate about Investing to use Slack in a compliant way. Refer to Appendix A for instructions on how to use Slack with and without an Investing account. 2.1.3 Email The sender or at least one of the recipients must be a @cashappinvest.com address. For example, if all participants have only an @block.xyz email address, this would be prohibited. The use of any personal email addresses is strictly prohibited. If no party to the email has a @cashappinvest.com email address, Cash App Investing Compliance (comms@cashappinvest.com) must be contacted before sending the email. Requesting an @cashappinvest.com Address: Employees are required to obtain a @cashappinvest.com email address if they regularly send or receive investing-related emails. Cash App Investing Complia

Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Afterpay Canada Limited (“Afterpay Canadaˮ, “Afterpay CAˮ) Anti-Money Laundering Program (“Programˮ) outlines Afterpay Canadaʼs approach to anti-money laundering, counter-terrorism financing, and sanctions compliance. The purpose of this Program is to establish comprehensive guidelines governing Afterpay Canada's operations to identify, mitigate, and manage money laundering and terrorism financing risks, while ensuring consistent operations and maintaining regulatory compliance. Afterpay Canada Limited, a British Columbia incorporated entity, offers "buy now, pay later" installment products in Canada. As a subsidiary of Afterpay Australia Pty., Afterpay Canada maintains this Program to meet subsidiary obligations under Australian AUSTRAC requirements, partner expectations from banking and financial institutions, and to implement voluntary best practices aligned with Canadian AML/CTF frameworks. Overview of the AML/CTF Program 1.1 About Afterpay CA Afterpay Canada Limited BN - BC1246445 (Afterpay CA, we, or us) is a reporting entity enrolled on the Reporting Entity Roll managed by AUSTRAC for the purposes of AML/CTF Laws. Afterpay CA is a subsidiary of Afterpay Pty Ltd, an indirect wholly owned subsidiary of Block, Inc. It is a non-bank institution that offers short-term loans (also referred to as ‘Low Cost Credit Contractsʼ or ‘Buy Now Pay Later Contractsʼ under the National Credit Code), but does not hold a banking licence. Afterpay CA offers a ‘Buy Now, Pay Laterʼ (BNPL) product service that allows a Customer to make an interest free purchases using a short-term, low value loans at a participating merchant and to repay the loan to Afterpay CA for them over four fortnightly instalments. Although Afterpay CA is registered, and operates in Canada (CA) and is subject to CA laws, the AML/CTF Law extend to the activities of a subsidiary of an Australian resident company insofar as that subsidiary provides a Designated Service at or through a permanent establishment of that subsidiary in a country other than Australia. Afterpay CA is a subsidiary of Afterpay Pty Ltd ACN 618 280 649, an Australian resident company, and it provides certain Designated Services at or through its permanent establishment in CA. Customers ordinarily fund the repayment of the instalments to Afterpay CA from debit or credit cards. Afterpay CA directly pays the merchant for the purchase price of the goods (less a fee), and assumes the credit risk. Afterpay CA settles these funds to the merchantʼs nominated bank account by providing instructions to its local bank partner to make those payments. documents.FC-010: Risk and Compliance Adverse Actions Policy Cash App Last Approved: Feb 4, 2026 Effective Date: Feb 4, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Risk and Compliance Adverse Actions Policy ("Policy") outlines Cash App's approach to actioning accounts and customers that violate Cash App's Terms of Service (herein referred to as "platform violations"), internal policies, or engage in suspicious or illegal activity (together, referred to as "prohibited activity"). This framework is designed to ensure Cash App has reasonable measures and appropriate methods to manage accounts and customers who present higher risk to Cash App, and to ultimately mitigate and manage the risks associated with potential money laundering, terrorist financing, fraud and other financial crimes in line with its BSA/AML and CTF obligations. The BSA/AML Program Office Lead is responsible for management of this Policy. Cash App conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Policy is to establish a holistic set of standards and requirements for which Cash App operates as it pertains to offboarding and/or putting certain limitations on customers and accounts. Scope: The scope of this Policy includes Risk and Compliance teams and processes that take action to accounts and customers on Cash App. Definitions: Terminology Definition Adverse Action Controls in place to limit customer access to Cash App as a result of prohibited activity. Account The structure that is used by Cash App customers to log in, manage their personal information, and send or receive money or engage in other transactions. Denylisting Accounts The process by which Cash App accounts are prevented from continued use, with the exception of liquidating assets and withdrawing funds after a determination or suspicion of involvement in prohibited, fraudulent or illegal activity on Cash App. Denylist Propagation The act of denylisting more than one account or identity-based asset with the intention of offboarding all accounts and assets linked to the account of a perpetrator of bad activity. Employee Identification A list of EINs updated and maintained by Compliance. Identity Based Assets An alias or attribute that a customer links to an account that may be associated with an identity (i.e., social security number, phone number, email address, financial instrument, device). Financial Instrument Linked external debit, prepaid, credit card and bank account Identity Verification The process of verifying a customer's identity, which may be ("IDV") triggered by a number of actions including but not limited to ordering a Cash Card, exceeding peer-to-peer sending and receiving limits, buying bitcoin or enabling bitcoin withdrawals and deposits, signing up for a Cash App Investing account, and sending from a stored balance Cash App Card Refers to the deactivation of a customer's Cash App Card and all Termination associated services, including the direct deposit feature. This ensures that the card cannot be reactivated or used for any future transactions. Risk Suspension A degraded customer experience state where certain features of Cash App are unavailable to the account until the customer or Cash App takes a particular action. Applied when certain risky customer behaviours are detected. documents.FC-011: Cash App Financial Crime Program Squareup Europe Limited SEL Last Approved: September 29, 2025 Effective Date: September 29, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Squareup Europe Limited (“SELˮ) is an authorised Electronic Money Institution, regulated and supervised by the Financial Conduct Authority (“FCAˮ) in the United Kingdom UK, under Firm Reference Number 900846. The direct parent company is Squareup International Limited, registered in the Republic of Ireland; both companies are wholly owned subsidiaries of Block, Inc. SELʼs two key product offerings are (i) Square Managed Payments and (ii) Cash App (in Winddown since October 2024. Further information on the products and services offered by SEL in the UK are located in Appendix 1. This Program document relates to the Cash App business. Block, Inc. Block, Inc., a Delaware corporation, is a merchant services aggregator and mobile payments company based in San Francisco, California in the United States of America. It is registered as a Money Services Business with the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (“FinCENˮ). SELʼs primary Cash App product offering is a peer-to-peer money transfer service, allowing individuals, through a mobile software application, to transfer funds via stored balance or from a debit card issued by a UK-based financial institution to another individualʼs Cash App account. A feature of Cash App is the ability to make cross-currency transactions (send and receive funds from other jurisdictions where Cash App is available). This feature enables individual Cash App users to transfer funds between Cash App accounts e.g., a US individual Cash App customer can transfer US dollars from their stored value balance or linked debit card to a UK individual customerʼs Cash App account where it will appear in her GBP stored value balance, and vice versa. Scope: This Financial Crime Program document applies to Cash App products available in the UK. Definitions: Terminology Definition Money Laundering Money Laundering is the process by which individuals or organisations conceal the origins of illegally obtained funds, making them appear legitimate. In the context of SEL, Money Laundering typically involves disguising the proceeds of crime through legitimate payment channels, including merchant transactions and card processing services. Criminals may attempt to exploit financial institutions to move, layer or integrate illicit funds into the financial system. As a regulated entity under the UK Money Laundering Regulations, SEL has a legal and ethical responsibility to detect and prevent such activities by implementing robust Anti-Money Laundering (“AMLˮ) controls, including customer due diligence (“CDDˮ), transaction monitoring and the reporting of suspicious activity. Terrorist Financing Terrorist Financing involves the collection or provision of funds with the intention or knowledge that they will be used to support terrorist acts, individuals or organisations. Unlike Money Laundering, the origin of the funds used for terrorist financing may be legitimate or illicit, making detection more complex. For SEL, there is a heightened risk that payment processing services could be misused to raise, move or store funds that support terrorism, often through seemingly legitimate merchants or transactions. Under the Terrorism Act 2000 (“TACTˮ) and related UK regulations, SEL must implement effective controls to identify and report suspicious activity, conduct enhanced due diligence (“EDDˮ) where necessary and ensure systems are in place to prevent its services from being exploited for terrorist financing purposes. Proliferation Financing Proliferation Financing refers to the act of providing funds or financial services that are used to support the development, acquisition or transfer of weapons of mass destruction (“WMDsˮ), including nuclear, chemical or biological weapons. This type of financing is often linked to sanctioned individuals, entities or countries and
documents.FC-012: Know Your Customer Policy Cash App US Last Approved: Feb 19, 2026 Effective Date: Feb 19, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The KYC Policy (“Policyˮ) is established by Cash App, U.S., a Line of Business under Block, Inc., and documents how Cash App knows the identity of its customers, understands the nature and purpose of customer relationships to develop a customer risk profile, reasonably predicts the types of transactions that a customer is likely to engage in, and determines when transactions are potentially suspicious. Cash App is required to adopt and implement a comprehensive KYC program to identify customers who may present a higher risk for money laundering, terrorist financing, reputational concern, and other types of illegal activity. This Policy is established by Cash App Compliance. References to “staffˮ throughout this document shall refer to all full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. Cash App conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Policy is to document Cash Appʼs requirements and framework for satisfying relevant KYC compliance obligations, and is intended to be supplemented by additional Policies, Standards, and Procedures where necessary. Further, it provides policy requirements around identity verification, risk-based due diligence and cadence, and periodic KYC reviews. Know Your Customer KYC compliance is integral in the prevention of identity theft, money laundering, terrorist financing, and other types of illegal activity, and begins with understanding a customerʼs identity and assessing the risks associated with the customer. Cash App employs a risk-based approach in order to form a reasonable belief that it knows the true identity of each customer. Scope: The Bank Secrecy Act, as amended and supplemented by the USA Patriot Act and other laws (collectively, the “BSAˮ), obligates covered financial institutions to implement anti-money laundering programs reasonably designed to prevent the institutions from being used to facilitate money laundering and the financing of terrorist activities. An essential component of a compliant AML program are policies and procedures designed to ensure the financial institution knows its customer. Therefore, the KYC Policy includes Cash Appʼs requirements for knowing and verifying the identity of customers, understanding the nature and purpose of customer relationships for the purpose of developing a customer risk profile, and conducting ongoing monitoring to identify and report suspicious transactions and, maintaining and updating customer information. Because Cash App engages in the transmission of funds, it is covered by the BSAʼs definition of “financial institutionˮ and subject to BSA requirements, including KYC requirements. Definitions: Terminology Definition Account The structure that is used by Cash App customers to log in, manage their personal information, and send or receive money or engage in other transactions. Bank Secrecy Act Passed in 1970 and amended several times, a U.S. law requiring financial institutions to assist U.S. government agencies in detecting and preventing money laundering. Customer A natural or juridical person that opens an account and receives Cash App products and services, or a person opening a new account for someone who lacks the legal capacity. A customer must receive services to be subject to the KYC Policy. Customer Due Diligence The information and documentation maintained by Cash App on its CDD or Enhanced Due accounts, customers, or relationships. Diligence EDD Profile Financial Institution Each agent, agency, branch, or office within the U.S. of any person doing business in one or more capacity as a bank, a broker or dealer in securities, a money service business, a casino, a commodities broker or dealer, among other things. Scope: This Standard is established by Clearpay UK, is applicable to Personnel of Clearpay Finance Ltd and sets out the approach to Consumer Due Diligence. Standard Requirements 2.1. Customer Identification and Verification 2.1.1 Timing Clearpay performs the customer identification and verification prior to providing any service to consumers for the first time, and at other times as specified in this Standard, including: ● in response to an increase in money laundering ML risk; and ● as part of an AML investigation. 2.1.2 Customer Types Clearpay only provides its services to UK-resident natural persons. Schedule 2 describes in detail the tiered procedure followed for the escalation of identity verification methods. 2.1.4 Customers that cannot provide satisfactory evidence Where a customer cannot provide satisfactory evidence of identity in accordance with this Standard due to exceptional circumstances, the case may be referred to the MLRO to determine whether the customer may be able to provide alternative documents to establish proof of identity. No alternative documents may be accepted as evidence of identity without approval from the MLRO. 2.1.5 Verification from reliable and independent electronic data Clearpay considers the verification procedures and underlying data sources used for eIDV of customers to be sufficiently extensive, reliable, accurate, independent of the customer, and capable of providing an appropriate level of assurance that the person claiming a particular identity is in fact that person. SELʼs primary product offering includes a combination of card acceptance hardware, made up of the Square Reader (a device used with an Apple iOS device or an Android mobile device), a Square Stand and an accompanying software application to provide the technical interface between the hardware and the merchant acquiring banking infrastructure. Collectively, this payment solution is referred to as "Square Managed Payments". Square Managed Payments reads debit, credit, gift, and prepaid card data and enables a merchant (an individual or a company) to accept non-cash payments using their iPhone/iPad/Android mobile device and send a digital receipt to their customer (or a printed receipt if compatible hardware is installed). Scope: This Financial Crime Program document applies to SEL products. Definitions: Terminology Definition Money Laundering Money Laundering is the process by which individuals or organisations conceal the origins of illegally obtained funds, making them appear legitimate. In the context of SEL, Money Laundering typically involves disguising the proceeds of crime through legitimate payment channels, including merchant transactions and card processing services. Criminals may attempt to exploit financial institutions to move, layer or integrate illicit funds into the financial system. As a regulated entity under the UK Money Laundering Regulations, SEL has a legal and ethical responsibility to detect and prevent such activities by implementing robust Anti-Money Laundering (“AMLˮ) controls, including customer due diligence (“CDDˮ), transaction monitoring and the reporting of suspicious activity. Terrorist Financing Terrorist Financing involves the collection or provision of funds with the intention or knowledge that they will be used to support terrorist acts, individuals or organisations. Unlike Money Laundering, the origin of the funds used for terrorist financing may be legitimate or illicit, making detection more complex. For SEL, there is a heightened risk that payment processing services could be misused to raise, move or store funds that support terrorism, often through seemingly legitimate merchants or transactions. Under the Terrorism Act 2000 (“TACTˮ) and related UK regulations, SEL must implement effective controls to identify and report suspicious activity, conduct enhanced due diligence (“EDDˮ) where necessary and ensure systems are in place to prevent its services from being exploited for terrorist financing purposes. Proliferation Proliferation Financing refers to the act of providing funds or financial Financing services that are used to support the development, acquisition or transfer of weapons of mass destruction (“WMDsˮ), including nuclear, chemical or biological weapons. This type of financing is often linked to sanctioned individuals, entities or countries and may involve complex networks designed to evade in
