Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Financial Crime Program (“Programˮ) is established by Squareup International Limited (“SILˮ). SIL is an authorised Electronic Money Institution, regulated and supervised by the Central Bank of Ireland (“CBIˮ) under Firm Reference Number C184117. The ultimate parent company is Block, Inc. (“Blockˮ), a Delaware corporation, which is a merchant services aggregator and mobile payments company based in San Francisco, California in the United States of America (“USˮ). It is registered as a Money Services Business with the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (“FinCENˮ). SIL has permission to provide services in the Economic European Area (“EEA") as set out in the European Union Payment Services) Regulations 2018. This arrangement is known as “passportingˮ. The key product offered by SIL is Square Managed Payments. SILʼs primary product offering includes a combination of card acceptance hardware, made up of the Square Reader (a device used with an Apple iOS device or an Android mobile device) and a Square Stand (a stand used exclusively with an Apple iPad) and an accompanying software application to provide the technical interface between the hardware and the merchant acquiring banking infrastructure. Collectively, this payment solution is referred to as "Square Managed Payments". Square Managed Payments reads debit, credit, gift, and prepaid card data and enables a Merchant (an individual or a company) to accept non-cash payments using their iPhone/iPad/Android mobile device and send a digital receipt to their Customer (or a printed receipt if compatible hardware is installed). SILʼs business is subject to a wide range of laws and regulations throughout the EEA and the US, including Irish anti-money laundering (“AMLˮ), countering the financing of terrorism (“CFTˮ), and financial sanctions (“FSˮ) legislation as well as the U.S. Treasury Departmentʼs Office of Foreign Assets Control (“OFACˮ) and sanctions requirements. In turn, SIL follows the international framework proposed by the Financial Action Task Force (“FATFˮ) and acts under guidance produced by the European Banking Authority (“EBAˮ) and the CBI. SIL is committed to developing, implementing, and maintaining an effective, risk-based AML/CFT program, which is reasonably designed to prevent SIL from being used to facilitate ML and the financing of terrorist activities. This Program document has been designed to meet the following objectives: ● To develop and implement a risk-based AML/CFT/FS program for all SIL activities in the European Union (“ EUˮ); ● To inform the development of risk-based AML/CFT/FS control procedures for all SIL activities in the EU; ● To promote awareness of applicable AML/CFT/FS laws and regulations; ● To detect, prevent, monitor and and report suspicious activity and any terrorist financing transactions; and ● To fully cooperate with all law enforcement, regulatory agencies, and financial intelligence units to the extent required and permitted under applicable law. Where relevant, SIL implements AML/CFT/FS measures as described in this Program on a pan-European basis. A regional EU country Annex provides an overview of the country regulations SIL adheres to in the countries in which it currently operates. This annex will be updated as and when SIL begins to provide regulated activities in other EEA countries. Scope: This program is established by SIL and is applicable to Seller. It is vital that all Staff members that support SIL are aware of, and are committed to complying with, all applicable requirements of this Program and relevant AML/CFT/FS laws and regulations. All Staff members are expected to maintain the standards specified within this Program. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Square Canada, Inc. (“Square Canadaˮ or “the Companyˮ) is a wholly owned subsidiary of Block, Inc., a Delaware corporation, which is a merchant services aggregator and mobile payments company in San Francisco, California. Square Canada is a money services business MSB offering payment processing solutions which allow customers (“Sellersˮ) to accept credit, debit and gift card payments in exchange for goods and services (“Managed Payments Service"). Square Canadaʼs Managed Payments Service includes a variety of product features, each of which is ancillary to the payment card processing solution1. Included within Square Canadaʼs Managed Payments Service are “crowdfunding platform servicesˮ, as defined under the PCMLTFA, which allow Sellers to raise funds from their respective customers. Crowdfunding platform services may only be used by Sellers to raise funds on their own behalf, and cannot be used to raise funds on behalf of persons or entities specified by them. Square Canada also offers prepaid business expense cards (where the source of funds is derived exclusively from payment processing activities accessed via Managed Payments Service), commercial loans, and merchant cash advances (collectively, “Financial Servicesˮ). Square Canada is committed to preventing its services from being used for illicit purposes such as money laundering and terrorist financing activities. Square Canada has established an anti-money laundering (“AMLˮ) and anti-terrorist financing (“ATFˮ) compliance program (“AML/ATF Programˮ) to ensure compliance with applicable AML and ATF laws and regulations. Accordingly, Square Canada has adopted this Anti-Money Laundering and Counter Terrorist Financing Policy (“AML/ATF Policyˮ, “this Policyˮ) and will conduct business activities in compliance with the principles found herein. 1 Managed Payments Service features include, but are not limited to: (i) Point of Sale; (ii) Invoices; (iii) Virtual Terminal; (iv) eCommerce; (v) Funds transfers to a sellerʼs external linked bank account; (vi) crowdfunding platform services; and (vii) Square Gift Cards. 1 Scope: This Policy is established by Square Canada and is applicable to Square Canadaʼs activities within Canada. documents.FC-023: U.S. Identity Verification Policy Square Last Approved: Dec 30, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This U.S. Identity Verification ("IDV") Policy ("Policy") is established by Square and applies to its U.S. operations. The Policy outlines Square's approach to IDV for each customer of Square's Financial Products. The KYC Program Manager or delegate is responsible for managing this Policy, with final approval from the Customer Onboarding and Appeals Program Lead. The purpose of this Policy is to facilitate compliance within all aspects of Block, Inc.'s Bank Secrecy Act ("BSA")/Anti-Money Laundering ("AML") Program, across Square's financial products, as well as its U.S. Know Your Customer ("KYC") Program Standard. Square's U.S. KYC Program Standard outlines the company's approach to ensuring the business reasonably knows its customers and assessing the risks involved in conducting business with them, including adherence to the Customer Identification Program ("CIP"). The IDV Policy details how customer information is collected and verified to establish identity as required by the CIP component of the KYC Program. Scope: This Policy is established by Square and is applicable to Square's Covered Products in the U.S. market. This Policy does not apply to products offered by other legal entities, including Square Financial Services, or other business units within Block, Inc., including Cash App, and Afterpay. Additionally, developer test accounts are outside the scope of this Policy. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Identity Verification ("IDV") Identity Verification ("IDV") is a risk-based method of collecting and confirming identity information to enable Square to form a reasonable belief that it knows the true identity of each customer. Covered Products Square defines its Covered Products and activities as products and services offered by Square that are required, whether due to regulatory, partner, or other obligations, to comply with Block, Inc.'s Bank Secrecy Act ("BSA")/Anti-Money Laundering ("AML") Program. Customer Identification A Customer Identification Program ("CIP"), a component of the Program ("CIP") Know Your Customer Program, is a requirement for United States ("U.S.") financial institutions to verify the identities of customers (both individuals and business entities) when they open new accounts. Beneficial Owner ("BO") Beneficial Owners ("BO") are individuals who either exercise substantial control over a legal entity customer or, directly or indirectly, own/control at least 25% of its ownership interests. 2. Policy Requirements Square is both directly and indirectly regulated in various jurisdictions according to the types of financial services and products offered and is subject to all applicable BSA/AML obligations. For the purposes of Square's U.S. products that do not require money transmission licenses, including its managed payments business, Square is not directly regulated. However, even where Square is not directly regulated, it is required to meet the same expectations of a regulated entity in commitment to its contractual obligations with acquiring partners. In order to fulfill these requirements with acquiring partners, Square must collect and verify certain information from its customers. Square's electronic IDV is performed automatically via direct application programming interface ("API") calls with vendors. 2 2.1 IDV Overview Before customers can use Square's financial products, Square verifies their identity using a combination of electronic identity verification ("eIDV") and document identity verification ("dIDV"), which are performed automatically via direct API calls with vendors. After Square verifies the customer's identity, their account is activated for payments processing. Only verified Square accounts can process payments and use Square's Financial Products. 2.2 Verification of Individuals and Sole Proprietors 2.2.1 Information Collection For iden
documents.FC-024: Bank Secrecy Act/Anti-Money Laundering & Countering the Financing of Terrorism Program Standard Block, Inc Last Approved: August 12, 2025 Effective Date: August 12, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Money Laundering and terrorist financing are serious financial crimes with potentially serious social and financial effects. The laws of the U.S. government impose affirmative obligations on covered entities to implement systems and processes to prevent, detect, and report on a range of illicit activity, including money laundering, terrorist financing, and other criminal acts. Block, Inc. and its subsidiaries (hereinafter referred to as "Block" or the "Company") are committed to complying with anti-money laundering ("AML") and countering the financing of terrorism ("CFT") laws and regulations in all jurisdictions in which they operate, and to preventing the misuse of services and any other related involvement in criminal or unlawful activity. This Bank Secrecy Act/Anti-Money Laundering & Countering the Financing of Terrorism Program Standard ("Standard") is designed to operationalize requirements under U.S. laws and regulations and meet the minimum standards set out under Block's Global AML/CFT Policy. This Standard also forms part of Block's Anti-Financial Crime domain under the Block Compliance Policy Governance Framework. Block complies with a number of AML/CFT laws and regulations globally including, but not limited to, the Bank Secrecy Act (“BSAˮ). The U.S. Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (“FinCENˮ), a bureau of the U.S. Department of the Treasury, issues and enforces regulations under the BSA. Among other things, the BSA requires financial institutions, such as money services businesses (“MSBsˮ), to develop, implement, and maintain an effective BSA/AML Compliance Program (“Programˮ or “BSA/AML Programˮ). Scope: This Standard is applicable to Block and all its U.S. subsidiaries. The only exception is Square Financial Services which is a Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation and has its own Compliance Program, which includes its own BSA/AML Program, and Cash App Investing LLC, a licensed money transmitter and registered money service business subject to the Bank Secrecy Act ("BSA") and USA PATRIOT Act. Cash App Investing maintains its independent AML/CFT Program while leveraging the resources, technology, and sophistication of the enterprise-wide Block AML Program. Certain other products may also be considered Covered Products, such as where required under partner bank expectation and/or contractual obligation. Each Business Line is responsible for maintaining a documented and up-to-date list of products and services that meet this definition. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Anti-Money Laundering & Counter Terrorist Financing Program (“Programˮ) outlines Cash App Investing LLCʼs (“CAI,ˮ “Broker-Dealer,ˮ or “Firmˮ) approach to comply with applicable laws, regulations, and guidelines to monitor, detect, prevent, and report possible money laundering, financial crimes and terrorist financing. The BSA/AML Compliance Officer (“Officerˮ) is responsible for management of this Program. This Program ensures compliance with regulations administered by the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (“FINRAˮ), the Bank Secrecy Act (“BSAˮ), and the Treasury Departmentʼs Office of Foreign Assets Control (“OFACˮ). This Program is risk-based and designed to address the risks of money laundering and terrorist financing as they relate to the characteristics of the customers CAI serves, where CAI customers are located, and the types of services CAI offers. CAI is a subsidiary of Block, Inc. (“Blockˮ), a licensed money transmitter and registered money service business subject to the Bank Secrecy Act (“BSAˮ) and USA PATRIOT Act requirements to establish and maintain an anti-money laundering (“AMLˮ) compliance program. While CAI maintains this independent Program, CAI leverages the resources, personnel, technology, and sophistication of the enterprise-wide Block AML program. The Firm is committed to substantive AML monitoring and continuous improvement in its AML program, including adequate oversight of Block enterprise-wide AML services. In both money laundering and terrorist financing, criminals may attempt to exploit loopholes and other weaknesses in the financial system to launder criminal proceeds or conduct other illegal activities. Both money laundering and terrorist financing are serious financial crimes with potentially devastating social and financial effects. Anti-Money Laundering and Counter Terrorist Financing Program | Cash App Investing LLC CAI reserves the right to investigate any known or suspected violation of this Program. Failure to comply with this Program may result in disciplinary action, including termination. Staff must be mindful that they may be subject to criminal liability if they are willfully blind to suspicious activity or should have known that an activity was suspicious. Willful blindness (deliberately ignoring facts that a reasonable person would consider suspicious) does not require active involvement in a money laundering scheme in order to create liability. CAI designed this Program to provide a framework for a robust AML program and to communicate at an organizational level CAIʼs commitment to a culture of effective AML compliance. This Program is intended to be supplemented by additional policies, programs and procedures where necessary. Some policies, programs or procedures are maintained by Block. References to “staff members'' throughout this document shall refer to all employees of CAI and/or Block and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Program, and include interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. documents.FC-029: Compliance Real Time Transaction Blocking Program Cash App | US Last Approved: Aug 19, 2025 Effective Date: Aug 19, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Cash App is committed to complying with applicable laws, regulations, and guidelines to monitor, detect, prevent, and report possible money laundering, financial crimes, and terrorist financing. The Real-time Transaction Blocking ("RTB") Program ("Program") is an additional component to the existing Transaction Monitoring ("TM") and Unacceptable Use Policy ("UUP") Programs. This Program provides a mechanism to evaluate attempted transactions in real-time, automatically preventing or blocking them if specific compliance concerns are detected. This is a 'preventive' program that complements the 'detective' TM and UUP programs. Any transactional activity not assessed by the RTB process will still be subject to detection by the downstream TM and UUP monitoring rules. This Program is established and maintained to help proactively reduce the overall risk of the Cash App platform by preventing suspected illicit, suspicious and/or terms of service violations activity from occurring on the platform. Cash App utilizes rules that consist of proprietary algorithms (machine-learning, heuristics or some combination thereof) to evaluate and block transactions in real-time. These rules are established and maintained by the Risk Machine Learning team. Any new rules or recommended changes to the existing rules are reviewed and approved by the Financial Crimes Detections program and in accordance with the Model Change Management Process. The Program is applicable to transaction blocking rules targeted at Compliance Concerns. Scope: This Program is established by Cash App and is applicable to all Cash App products in the United States. Definitions: Terminology Definition Real time Transaction Blocking A system that evaluates a transaction in flight and makes a ("RTB") System decision to block transaction based on the rule output. Good Customer Any user of the platform that is not involved in perpetrating fraud, scam, illegal/suspicious activity or Terms of Service violations in any form or fashion. Good Transaction Any transaction on the platform that is not involved in perpetrating fraud, scam, illegal/suspicious activity or Terms of Service violations in any form or fashion. Customer) Insult Any negative action on a good customer account or action that ends up negatively impacting that customers' experience or ability to transact on the platform. Compliance Concern A type of bad activity that Compliance actively monitors/seeks to prevent. (e.g., illegal drug sales, money laundering, adult services). Staff Member All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Program, and include interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. 2. Program Requirements 2.1 Process Presentation Fig. 1 - Real-time transaction evaluation and blocking system Fig. 2 - Offline rule performance measurement loop 2.2 Transaction Evaluation, Blocking and Attribution RTB is specific to a particular payment type (e.g., a fiat peer to peer payment or a fiat remittance payment type). Additionally, different rules may be targeted at one or more Compliance Concerns (e.g., Prostitution prevention rule or a Drug trafficking prevention rule) for the same payment type. Multiple rules may even target the same Compliance Concern. Therefore, this Program will include multiple rules across multiple payment types and Compliance Concerns combined. Each payment type may have multiple rule evaluations and each rule can make a decision to block independently of other rules. When multiple rules evaluate a payment type, the decision to block will be true if any of the rules decide to block the payment (aka OR mechanism). If multiple rules make a decision to block, the block will be attributed to the rule corresponding to the high
documents.FC-031: Identity Verification Standard Cash App US Last Approved: Dec 15, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 15, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Identity Verification Standard (“Standardˮ) is established by Cash App, and is applicable to the Cash App business in the United States. This Standard is designed to define Cash Appʼs strategy to obtain and verify a customerʼs identity. Identity Verification (“IDVˮ) is a process that allows Cash App to maintain compliance with its Know Your Customer (“KYCˮ) Policy, which establishes Cash Appʼs requirements for complying with applicable laws, regulation, and guidelines to monitor, detect, and prevent identity theft, money laundering, terrorist financing, and other types of illegal or illicit activity. In order to protect customers, prevent identity theft, and combat other fraud risk, Cash App should not allow the continued use of a Cash App account if there are concerns related to the authenticity of the identity submitted during the IDV process. Concerns around authenticity may include but are not limited to: synthetic identities, the fraudulent use of another personʼs identity, and use of fraudulent, forged, or inauthentic documents. If these red flags are identified after onboarding and verification, the account should be handled with the appropriate corresponding adverse action. Cash App conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Standard is to document Cash Appʼs strategy to ensure adherence to laws and regulations as outlined in the Cash App KYC Policy. Scope: This Standard is established by Cash App and is applicable to all subsidiaries used for the Cash App business, or Cash App subsidiary in the United States. documents.FC-033: Know Your Customer Standard Cash App US Last Approved: Dec 23, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 23, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: In accordance with the Block, Inc. (herein referred to as the "Company" or "Block") BSA/AML Policy, the US Cash App ("Cash") Know Your Customer ("KYC") Standard has been established to ensure that Cash App reasonably understands who its customers are, and the risks posed by those customers. The Customer Onboarding and Management team ("COM") has been established to oversee implementation and monitoring of KYC processes, controls and procedures. Compliance is responsible for implementing procedures to ensure proper information or documentation is obtained and stored for each customer file. A risk-based approach is used to ensure customers identified as posing a heightened risk are subject to Enhanced Due Diligence ("EDD"), allowing the business to further understand the risks the customer poses and establish processes to mitigate those risks. The cornerstone of a strong BSA/AML compliance program is the adoption and implementation of risk-based due diligence policies, procedures, and processes for all customers, particularly those that present a higher risk for money laundering and terrorist financing. In order to achieve this, Cash App has designed and implemented a customer risk rating program that assesses a customer's profile, assigns risk ratings, and monitors the customers' risk levels. The customer risk rating ("CRR") program enables Cash App to apply the appropriate risk-based controls to mitigate exposure to money launderers, terrorist financing, and financial crime. Improper assessment of a customer's risk can have a cascading effect, creating deficiencies in multiple areas of internal controls and resulting in an overall weakened AML compliance program. The KYC program captures Customer Due Diligence ("CDD"), Enhanced Due Diligence ("EDD"), Periodic Due Diligence, and Ongoing Due Diligence to ensure Cash App has a clear understanding of its customer base and continuously evaluates risks associated with money laundering, terrorist financing and other financial crimes. It also establishes consistent risk-based monitoring controls on the platform, including KYC, Know Your Business ("KYB"), Politically Exposed Person ("PEP"), Source of Wealth/ Source of Funds ("SoW/SoF"), Adverse media screening, KYC Refresh and Section 314(a) screening. Scope: All Cash App Compliance personnel should be knowledgeable in program content and are responsible for compliance with this Program in their related business activities. Cash App employees should have access to policies that affect their jobs and understand their responsibilities to perform their work in an ethical and legally compliant manner. Business units engaged in the development of new products and services, whether resulting from internal product development or merger and acquisition activities, must consider this Program. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The Anti-Money Laundering and Counter Terrorism Financing AML/CTF Risk Awareness Training Program (the “Programˮ) is a mandated part of Square AU Pty Ltdʼs (“Square AUˮ) AML/CTF Program , as required by AUSTRAC under the AML/CTF Act. It is central to Square AUʼs effort to protect its business from being used to facilitate money laundering, terrorism financing and proliferation financing (“PFˮ). The purpose of this Program is to equip Employees with the necessary knowledge and understanding to effectively identify and manage risks associated with money laundering, terrorism financing and proliferation financing. documents.FC-036: Employee and Personnel Due Diligence Program Square | Square AU Pty Ltd Last Approved: Mar 26, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 26, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The Employee and Personnel Due Diligence Program (the “Programˮ) details Square AUʼs risk based approach to screen employees and personnel to minimise the risks that they may commit, or assist others in committing money laundering, financing of terrorism (“ML/TFˮ) or other financial crime offences. The purpose of this Program is to establish and maintain an effective framework for assessing and managing the risks associated with employing or engaging individuals who may be involved in activities that could facilitate money laundering, terrorist financing, or proliferation financing. This program is designed to ensure that all personnel , particularly those in roles that influence AML/CTF outcomes or have access to sensitive financial information or decision-making authority over high-risk activities, undergo thorough background checks and risk-based ongoing monitoring. By identifying and mitigating potential risks early, the program helps protect Square AU from reputational, legal, and financial harm, while ensuring compliance with the AML/CTF Act and supporting the ongoing effectiveness of Square AUʼs AML/CTF controls. Scope: The Program applies to all employees (including full time, part time and casual workers) and directors (collectively known as “employeesˮ) of Square AU, and personnel (including individuals employed or otherwise engaged by Square AU who perform functions relevant to its AML/CTF obligations). Where individuals are engaged through employment agencies or other third-party arrangements, Square AU satisfies its personnel due diligence obligations through contractual screening requirements and oversight mechanisms agreed with the relevant agency or provider. Due diligence procedures, including background checks appropriate to the AML/CTF risk of the role, are conducted by the employment agency pursuant to the applicable services agreement. Square AU reserves the right to review, test and audit the agencyʼs screening procedures and requires hiring managers to attest that screening proportionate to the risk of the role has been completed prior to commencement. The Program applies when Square AU transfers contractors into permanent roles as direct hires of Square AU. Program Requirements Square AU adopts a risk-based approach to Employee and Personnel Due Diligence. Personnel performing functions relevant to Square AUʼs AML/CTF obligations are subject to formal due diligence requirements proportionate to their level of financial crime risk, regulatory exposure, and decision-making authority. In addition, to address risks arising from broader organisational structures and cross-functional responsibilities, Square AU applies baseline due diligence measures across all employees to support consistent risk management, governance, and oversight. The Program defines the triggers of ECDD and the actions Square AU takes when ECDD is performed. It also documents additional requirements from Acquirers and Payment Networks. The program sets out the ECDD measures applied in response to specific ML/TF risks. It also describes how the effectiveness of ECDD measures is monitored and reviewed, and outlines how Square AU manages its tipping-off obligations when conducting ECDD. Scope: This Program is established by Square AU and is applicable to the Square AU products through which Square AU provides Designated Services as defined under the AML/CTF Act. This Program is implemented, managed and maintained by the Square AU Compliance team and sets out the approach to design and execution Square AUʼs ECDD Program. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The Financial Crimes Transaction Monitoring Program TMP is a key component of Square AU Pty Ltd's ("Square AU") Anti-Money Laundering & Counter-Terrorism Financing Program, designed to meet the obligations set out by the Australian Transaction Report and Analysts Centre AUSTRAC. The Financial Crime Transaction Monitoring Program for Square AU is overseen by the Square AU CCO, who provides approval and ensures regulatory compliance. The AML/CTF Compliance Officer is responsible for managing and updating the program when required, ensuring it adheres to local regulatory requirements. In accordance with Square AU Anti-Money Laundering & Counter-Terrorism Financing Program, the TMP establishes methods to monitor, detect, and prevent possible money laundering, financial crimes, terrorist financing and proliferation financing for Square AU's customer transactions in Australia. The program is designed to identify, mitigate and manage money laundering, terrorism financing and proliferation financing risks in compliance with AUSTRAC's requirements and the AML/CTF Act. Its primary objective is to identify and report suspicious transactions, including but not limited to those that deviate from a customer's normal behaviour, involve high-risk jurisdiction, or trigger specific red-flag indicators. Scope: This Program is established by Square AU Pty Ltd Square AU and applicable to Square AU products and services, such as Square Point of Sale ("POS") and other financial products that process transactions which are deemed to fall within the scope of this Program. It is implemented, managed and maintained by the Square Australian AU Compliance team and sets out the approach to design and execution Square AU's Transaction Monitoring program. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Anti-Money Laundering Set of laws, regulations and procedures designed to detect and ("AML") deter money laundering, terrorist financing, and any other financial crimes. Under section 5 and 28 of the AML/CTF Act and the AML/CTF Rules, reporting entities must implement appropriate risk-based systems and controls to identify and manage the risks associated with customers who are PEPs, including their family members and close associates. The PEP Watchlist Program for Square AU (“Programˮ) is overseen by the Square AU CCO, who provides necessary approval and ensures regulatory compliance. documents.FC-043: U.S. Know Your Customer Standard Square | US Last Approved: Jan 6, 2026 Effective Date: Jan 6, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The U.S. Know Your Customer ("KYC") Standard ("Standard") outlines Square's KYC Program ("KYC Program") to ensure the business reasonably knows its customers and the associated risks of conducting business with its customers. The KYC Program Lead is responsible for managing this Standard, in accordance with Block, Inc.'s Bank Secrecy Act ("BSA")/Anti-Money Laundering ("AML") Program. Square is committed to maintaining full compliance with the laws and regulations that govern its business. The U.S. KYC Standard covers Square's approach to Customer Identification, Customer Due Diligence ("CDD"), and Enhanced Due Diligence ("EDD"). The Standard is committed to identifying customers who may present a higher risk for money laundering, terrorist financing, identity theft, reputational risk, or other types of illegal activity. Square's U.S. KYC Program Standard enables the business to: ● Know and verify the identity of its customers; ● Understand the nature and purpose of customers' account activity and apply a Customer Risk Rating (“CRRˮ) score; ● Apply enhanced due diligence to higher risk customers; ● Understand the customer's business and expected transactions for each customers' profile; and ● Conduct ongoing customer due diligence ("OCDD") to confirm if customerʼs information is accurate. Square conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations, in addition to network, partnership requirements and Square's Terms of Service and Payment Terms. The purpose of the U.S. KYC Standard is to ensure that Square reasonably knows its customers and the associated risks of conducting business with its customers and adheres to the Block AML & CTF Policy and related Standard. Scope: This Standard is established by Block, Inc. and is applicable to Square's U.S. products and services. Square uses risk-based controls to evaluate a broad range of customers, which are businesses. This includes sellers that use Square for merchant payment services, including, e-commerce, inventory, appointments, invoicing, customer messaging, and products offered in conjunction with banks such as Square Checking and Square Card. Customer Due Diligence The process through which Square reviews seller accounts to ("CDD") understand the nature and purpose of the customer's account activity. documents.GOV-001: Controlling the Assault of Non-Solicited Pornography and Marketing Act (“CANSPAMˮ) Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The Controlling the Assault of Non-Solicited Pornography And Marketing Act (“CANSPAMˮ) Policy demonstrates Block, Inc.'s commitment to comply with the CANSPAM Act across all its subsidiaries and operations. This policy establishes comprehensive guidelines governing Block's commercial email practices while maintaining brand-specific flexibility where needed. The CANSPAM Act serves as a crucial regulatory framework enacted to reduce spam and unsolicited pornography by prohibiting senders of unsolicited commercial email messages from disguising the source and content of their messages, while ensuring consumers maintain control over their email communications. CANSPAM requires Block to follow specific procedures when initiating email messages to customers or prospective customers, including obtaining affirmative consent, ensuring message content is not false or misleading, and providing recipients with the ability to opt-out of future correspondence. Scope: This Policy applies to Block, Inc. and its applicable U.S. subsidiaries and affiliates (collectively, "Block") that send commercial email messages. The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. It governs all commercial emails sent by or on behalf of Block, including messages with both commercial and transactional content, and third-party email services. This Policy applies to Square, Cash App, and Afterpay's commercial email communications but does not apply to purely transactional messages or internal communications. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Affirmative As it relates to commercial electronic mail ("Email") messages, Affirmative Consent Consent occurs when the Recipient expressly consents to receive the message, either in response to a clear and conspicuous request for such consent or at the Recipient's own initiative. If the message is from a party other than the party to which the Recipient communicated such consent, the Recipient must have been given clear and conspicuous notice at the time the consent was communicated that their Email address could be transferred to such other party for the purpose of initiating Commercial Email Messages. Commercial Any Email message the Primary Purpose of which is to advertise or promote Email Message for a commercial purpose, a commercial product or service including content on the Internet. An Email message would not be considered to be a Commercial Email Message solely because such a message includes a reference to a commercial entity that serves to identify the Sender or a reference or link to an Internet website operated for a commercial purpose. Primary Purpose The Primary Purpose of an Email message will be deemed to be commercial if it: ● Contains only the commercial advertisement or promotion of a commercial product or service ● Contains both commercial content and "transactional or relationship" content if either: ○ A Recipient reasonably interpreting the subject line of the Email message would likely conclude that the message contains commercial content; or ○ The Email message's "transactional or relationship" content does not appear in whole or substantial part at the beginning of the body of the message ● Contains both commercial content as well as content that is not transactional or relationship content if a Recipient reasonably interprets either: ○ The subject line of the Email message would likely conclude that the message contains commercial content; or ○ The body of the message would likely conclude that the Primary Purpose of the message is commercial Recipient An authorized user of the Email address to which the message was sent or delivered. 2 Sende

Overview Introduction and Purpose: Afterpay Canada (“Afterpayˮ) is a British Columbia incorporated entity that offers “buy now, pay laterˮ installment products in Canada. Block will comply with the New York State Department of Financial Services’s (NYDFS) Part 504 provisions as well as other international laws, regulations, and expected standards and accordingly maintain this Model Validation Policy (the “Policy”), which will oversee Block’s model governance framework and provide guidance on how Block will incorporate the following practices, for the anti-money laundering (AML) transaction monitoring models and watchlist screening and filtering technologies: ● On an annual basis, Block elects to perform a coverage assessment on applicable key financial crimes models to test against expected levels of conceptual soundness; ● Block performs periodic reviews and testing of the accuracy of its transaction monitoring and filtering and screening systems as well as their alignment to the Global AML and Counter-Terrorist Financing Policy and the Global Sanctions Policy, and their corresponding key programs; ● Block maintains key model, system, and data-related documentation for Block’s AML transaction monitoring system and watchlist screening and filtering systems1; ● Block maintains robust oversight through mandated review and approval processes for any changes to its transaction monitoring system and watchlist screening and filtering systems, from the relevant money laundering reporting officers (MLROs) and delegates of the Bank Secrecy Act (“BSA”)/AML Compliance Officer; and ● Block elects to conduct an independent assessment to assist the BSA/AML Compliance Officer in accurately attesting for the annual NYDFS Part 504 certifications. This Policy is established by Block and is applicable globally across all Block business lines, subsidiaries, and jurisdictions. Model Tuning A subset of model validation, which is a process to determine that a defined model is optimized to deliver effective results, per predefined performance metrics. documents.GOV-013: Business Activity Monitoring Policy Block, Inc. | Cash App Last Approved: Aug 12, 2025 Effective Date: Aug 12, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The Business Activity Monitoring Policy (“Policyˮ) defines Cash App's approach to identifying business activity conducted in Personal Accounts, along with corresponding action taken. This Policy is critical to ensuring our customers are using the correct account type based on their intended use of Cash App. The Cash App Product team is responsible for complying with this Policy. Scope: This Policy is established by Cash App and is applicable to all Block entities used for the Cash App business. Cash App facilitates peer-to-peer ("P2P") payments, enabling customers to send fiat currency from one account to another. Payments can be funded by the customerʼs Cash App balance or linked instruments, such as debit or credit cards. Cash App accounts can generally be used for either personal or business use. To remain compliant with this Policy, customers must use the appropriate account type based on the nature of their transactions. This is critical to ensuring we are able to continuously and effectively monitor customer accounts and transactions, understand our relationship with our customers, and ensure we are able to maintain compliance with relevant tax reporting obligations. Personal accounts are for personal, non-commercial use, while payments for goods and services require a C4B account. Definitions: Terminology Definition Business BAM is a process designed to monitor, detect, and if necessary action Activity personal accounts operating as a Business. Monitoring (“BAMˮ) BAM Team Employee(s), which includes Engineering, Product, Compliance, Legal, Design, and Risk Machine Learning, who support the BAM program. Business A Business is generally defined as an individual or entity accepting payments for goods and services. Cash App for A C4B account is designed to support certain types of businesses providing Business goods or services to their consumers. In order to open a C4B account, users (“C4Bˮ) are required to provide certain information about the business in order to Account verify the identity of the business. Personal A Personal Account is designed to allow Cash App customers to send or Account receive funds for personal, non-commercial purposes. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1. Cash for Business Accounts Generally, a Business can be defined as an individual or entity providing a good or service to consumers in exchange for money. On Cash App, if a customer uses their account for selling goods or services, they must create a C4B account. In order to open a C4B account, a user is required to provide certain information about themselves and their Business in order to verify and open the account. To mitigate the risk of customers using their personal account for business activity, Cash App has established a Business Activity Monitoring (“BAMˮ) system. 2.2. BAM System The BAM system is a coordinated effort across Product, Engineering, Design, Risk and Compliance designed to monitor, detect, and if necessary, take action on personal accounts primarily being used for business purposes. This approach may leverage a combination of machine learning models, heuristics, and/or other data intelligence to identify suspected business operating personal accounts and take action commensurate with the level of confidence in the underlying accountʼs activity. The outcome of these detections may result in directing potentially non-compliant personal accounts toward C4B onboarding, provided their business activities are legally permissible and do not fall within Section “4. Unsupported Industries" of the Cash For Business Payment Terms. 2.3. Implementation Date and Transition Period To enforce this policy, Cash App has developed a risk-based Business Account Migration BAM system with the requirements outlined in this Policy effective September 2025. documents.GOV-015: Outsourcing Policy Squareup Europe Limited Approved: September 29, 2025 Effective Date: September 29, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Outsourcing Policy is established by Squareup Europe Limited (“SELˮ). SEL is an authorised Electronic Money Institution, regulated and supervised by the Financial Conduct Authority (“FCAˮ) under Firm Reference Number 900846. The direct parent company is Squareup International Limited (“SILˮ), registered in the Republic of Ireland; both SEL and SIL are wholly owned subsidiaries of Block, Inc (“Blockˮ). Block Inc. a Delaware corporation, and a merchant services aggregator and mobile payments company based in San Francisco, California in the United States of America. It is registered as a Money Services Business with the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (“FinCENˮ). Outsourcing involves the use of a third party service provider in any number of operational functions to perform ongoing activities that would normally be undertaken by SEL personnel. Outsourcing Arrangements generate risks for SEL. These risks must be identified and relevant controls applied prior to SEL entering into the arrangement, and these risks must be effectively managed throughout the outsourcing lifecycle. The purpose of this Policy (“the Policyˮ, “this Policyˮ) is to document SELʼs approach to doing this, aligned with applicable legal and regulatory requirements and relevant supervisory guidance. As a member of the Block Inc. Group, SEL is subject to the Third Party Risk Management Policy, which ensures that all third party vendors are reviewed and assessed in accordance with such policy at the Block level. This Policy should be read in conjunction with the Block Third Party Risk Management Policy. Where there is a conflict between the Block Third Party Risk Management Policy and this Policy, this Policy will prevail. SELʼs Senior Management and the Board recognise that the delegation of activities under an Outsourcing Arrangement does not result in any delegation of responsibility, and that ultimate responsibility for outsourced processes, services, or activities remains with SEL management and the SEL Board at all times. The purpose of this Policy is to set out SELʼs approach to the planning, implementation, monitoring and management of ‘Outsourcingʼ of activities by SEL to Outsourcing Service Providers (“OSPsˮ). Scope: This Policy is established by SEL and is applicable to all SEL Outsourcing Arrangements and SEL Staff Members. Definitions: Terminology Definition Critical Activity Functions that are necessary to perform core business lines or critical business functions, unless the institutionʼs assessment establishes that a failure to provide the outsourced function or the inappropriate provision of the outsourced function would not have an adverse impact on the operational continuity of the core business line or critical business function1. Intra-group Outsourcing Refers to an Outsourcing Arrangement between SEL and any entity within Block. Outsourcing Arrangement An arrangement between SEL and a vendor (whether intra-group or an external third party) by which the service provider performs a process, a service or an activity, or parts thereof, that would otherwise be undertaken by SEL, even if SEL has not performed that process, service or activity in the past. Outsourcing Framework/Program Any references to “Outsourcing Frameworkˮ or “Outsourcing Programˮ refer to this Policy, the SEL Outsourcing Procedures documents and the SEL Outsourcing Strategy. The terms “Outsourcing Programˮ and “Outsourcing Frameworkˮ are used interchangeably across this document. Outsourcing Risk Management Refers to the framework in place to identify, assess and manage risks associated with Outsourcing Arrangements. Outsourced Service Provider Are considered as a third-party entity that is undertaking ("OSP") an outsourced process, service or activity, or parts thereof, under an outsourcing arrangement on behalf of SEL. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Square AU Pty Ltd (“Square AUˮ) is an Australian Financial Services License AFSL holder License Number: 513929. This Breach Reporting Policy (“Policyˮ) outlines Square AUʼs approach to reporting a breach. The Chief Compliance Officer (“CCOˮ) Square AU is responsible for management of this Policy. This Policy is reviewed at least annually, and after any major regulatory change or incidents, by the Square AU Compliance team and approved by the Square AU Board. The Policy applies to all Square AU operations, Employees, and relevant third party service providers. In accordance with ASIC's Regulatory Guide 78 RG 78 and Square AU Pty Ltd's obligations under the Corporations Act, this Breach Reporting Policy outlines the framework for identifying, assessing, escalating, and reporting breaches within the organisation. As an Australian Financial Services AFS licensee, Square AU is mandated to report certain incidents, known as "Reportable Situations," to ASIC. This policy details the roles and responsibilities across the organisation, providing clear guidance on breach significance, timing, documentation and escalation to ensure compliance and protect stakeholders. Square AU conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Policy is to further Square AUʼs Breach reporting practices by providing for processes and guidelines to manage breaches within Square AU. Scope: This Policy, which sets out Square AUʼs approach to breach reporting under the Australian Financial Services Regime, is established by Square AU and is applicable to the Employees of Square AU. It also applies to: ● All persons engaged by Square AU (whether permanent, contract, or via third-party service providers), ● All relevant breaches affecting Square AUʼs regulated product or service footprint in Australia, and ● Any reporting required by contractual obligation to banking or business partners where regulatory breach reporting is specified. Definitions: Terminology Definition Reportable A ‘Reportable Situationʼ can fall into two categories: Situation ● Category 1 A ‘Core Reportable Situationʼ arises in any of the following scenarios: ○ Square Au Pty. Ltd. (“Square AUˮ) has breached a ‘Core Obligationʼ and the breach is ‘Significantʼ; ○ Square AU is no longer able to comply with a ‘Core Obligationʼ and the breach, if it occurs, will be ‘Significantʼ; ○ Square AU conducts an ‘Investigationʼ1 into whether there is a Reportable Situation, and the Investigation continues for more than 30 days2; or ○ an Investigation discloses there is no Reportable Situation ● Category 2 Gross negligence or fraud: A Category 2 Reportable Situation may arise if: ○ in the course of providing a financial service, Square AU has engaged in conduct constituting gross negligence; ○ Square AU has committed serious fraud3; or ○ any other circumstances prescribed by the regulations for the purposes of this paragraph (none are prescribed). Core Obligation ‘Core Obligationsʼ mean the obligations imposed on Square AU as an AFS licensee in ss 912A and 912B of the Corporations Act4 Investigation Square AU conducts an ‘Investigationʼ5 if: ● there has been some information gathering or human effort applied by Square AU to determine whether a breach has occurred or will 1 The term ‘Investigationʼ is not defined in the legislation and has its ordinary meaning. documents.GOV-019: Compliance Management System CMS Policy Square AU Last Approved: Dec 18, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 18, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Compliance Management System CMS Policy (the Policy) outlines Square AU Pty Ltdʼs (Square AU) approach to identifying, assessing, managing, monitoring, and reporting regulatory and ethical compliance obligations and risks across its Australian operations. The CMS promotes integrity, accountability, transparency, and continuous improvement and is aligned to global compliance practices. Scope: This Policy applies to all Square AU operations, officers, employees, and contractors engaged in regulated and supporting business activities. 1 Square AU operates within a broad and dynamic Australian regulatory environment that includes financial services, financial crime, consumer protection, and payments obligations. While the CMS considers this entire regulatory landscape, it applies a risk-based approach to prioritise programs and controls proportional to the nature, scale, and complexity of Square AUʼs operations. This approach ensures resources are directed to the areas of greatest regulatory, operational, and reputational risk, while maintaining visibility of broader obligations and emerging developments. Key areas of focus under this Policy include: ● AFSL and Conduct Obligations under the Corporations Act 2001 Cth, ASIC Regulatory Guides and Anti Bribery and Corruption; ● Financial Crime Compliance under the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006 Cth and AUSTRAC Rules and Autonomous Sanctions Act 2011Cth; ● Consumer Protection under ASIC, ACCC, and relevant industry codes; ● Complaints Handling, including requirements under ASIC Regulatory Guide 271 and the AFCA Rules; and ● Payments Industry Compliance, encompassing scheme participation, network standards, and relevant prudential or operational guidelines. Square AU operates as an independent Australian licensee within the Block group, aligning its local CMS with Blockʼs enterprise-wide compliance standards and programs to ensure consistency, accountability, and effective global governance. Global Alignment (summary): Square AUʼs CMS is supported by Block Inc.ʼs global compliance ecosystem (e.g., Global Monitoring & Testing Program, Issue Management Program, PoP, TPRM Standard). This ensures enterprise consistency and shared tooling while Square AU retains full accountability to Australian regulators and its Board. Definitions: AFSL Australian Financial Services Licence): A licence granted under the Corporations Act 2001 Cth authorising entities to provide specified financial services in Australia. The AFSL framework establishes the licenseeʼs obligations to maintain organisational competence, manage conflicts, ensure compliance, and act efficiently, honestly, and fairly. Responsible Manager: An individual nominated under the AFSL to demonstrate the organisationʼs competence and integrity in meeting its licensing obligations (refer ASIC Regulatory Guide 105). Responsible Managers play a key role within the compliance management system by ensuring controls, governance, and oversight arrangements align with licence conditions and regulatory expectations. 2 Compliance Management System CMS: A structured and integrated framework encompassing governance, risk management, compliance policies, procedures, controls, monitoring, training, reporting, and assurance mechanisms designed to ensure and evidence adherence to legal, regulatory, and ethical obligations. The CMS supports proactive identification and management of compliance risks, continuous improvement, and a culture of integrity across the organisation. Issue: A deficiency, weakness, or non-conformity in a process, control, or compliance obligation that may impair compliance effectiveness. Issues are logged, risk-rated, and tracked to remediation within the CMS to ensure accountability and timely resolution. Significant Breach: A breach that

documents.GOV-022: Compliance Management System CMS Policy Square | Square Capital, LLC | Credit Card Last Approved: October 3, 2025 Effective Date: October 3, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Compliance Management System ("CMS") Policy ("Policy") outlines Square Capital, LLC's ("Square Capital" or the "Company") commitment to implementing and maintaining an effective CMS. The Head of Block Consumer Protection Compliance and/or designee is responsible for management of this Policy. The purpose of this Policy is to set forth the expectations of the Square Capital Board of Managers ("SCBM") for managing and mitigating applicable regulatory compliance risk as it pertains to the Square Credit Card product. This Policy sets forth the framework within which Square Capital implements its CMS. The SCBM recognizes that maintaining an effective CMS is critical for mitigating the risks arising from violations of law and regulations and the potential for customer harm and is necessary to maintain a high level of public confidence in Square Capital's safe and sound operating practices. A critical aspect of managing and mitigating regulatory compliance risk is the development and maintenance of a CMS that is integrated into the overall framework for product design, delivery, and administration of all product and service life cycles. In general, Square Capital achieves this standard by ensuring a sound compliance structure is part of daily operations and that the Company identifies Issues and initiates appropriate and timely remediations. Square Capital's commitment to consumer protection is underscored by its dedication to accountability, transparency, and business ethics. It is Square Capital's policy to comply with all applicable federal state laws and regulations. Pursuant to this Policy, Square Capital will take prudent and reasonable steps to ensure compliance, including careful consideration of applicable commercial credit card laws and regulations. Scope: This Policy is applicable to the Square Capital Credit Card product only. Definitions: Terminology Definition Issue An Issue is defined as a deficiency gap, or weakness in the design, performance, or operating effectiveness. Staff Member An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labor, contractors) hired by Square Capital who supports the operations covered by this Policy. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1 Requirements for the CMS The SCBM recognizes that sound compliance risk management, like other areas of operational management, depends on Square Capital establishing a comprehensive program of risk controls and reviews. Square Capital requires the CMS to be tailored to Square Capital's organizational needs, business strategy, operational complexity, product diversity, market circumstances, regulatory environment, and risk profile. Because these essential criteria are not static, the SCBM also requires that the CMS have a strong infrastructure while remaining dynamic in its ability to respond to changes. The SCBM mandates that Square Capital's CMS ● Define roles and responsibilities for regulatory compliance activities covered by Square Capital's CMS; ● Require the creation and maintenance of policies, procedures, and controls that ensure that Square Capital conducts business in compliance with applicable laws and regulations and in a manner that meets customer service goals; ● Set forth internal supervision of Square Capital systems in order to deliver consistent and on-going execution of CMS standards; ● Conduct an annual compliance risk assessment to evaluate inherent risks, controls, and the resulting residual risks to Square Capital in order to guide the execution of the CMS; ● Create a monitoring and testing program, and separate independent audit requirements to identify operational and programmatic deficiencies; ● Establish change management and corrective action programs; ● Outline training requirements for all Staff, including the SCBM; ● Distribute compliance reporting f

(the “Company” or “Block”) engages in several money transmission, payment activities, investment, and lending services which are regulated. Block maintains money transmission or electronic money institution licenses globally and as such is subject to examinations, audits and independent assessments. Block must also complete assessments for various aspects of its business to ensure we are meeting our legal, regulatory, and business partner requirements for compliance. This Audit, Exam, and Independent Assessment Management Program (the “Program”) articulates Block’s approach to managing and supporting various audits, regulatory examinations, and independent assessments. It outlines the roles and responsibilities of stakeholders to promote transparency, consistency, and collaboration. This program is established by Block, Inc. (“Block”) and is applicable globally across all Block business lines, subsidiaries, and jurisdictions. References to “staff” throughout this document shall refer to all full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. Definitions Regulatory examinations A regulatory examination is a review of a financial institution to determine whether the financial institution is compliant with the local laws and regulations. Block is subject to examinations of various aspects of its business to ensure it meets licensing and regulatory requirements. Independent assessments An Independent assessment is a review of Block’s Compliance program (either in whole or in part) as directed by contractual agreements or specific regulatory requirements. This may include bank partner audits, network and acquirer partner audits. Engagement A term used to describe a regulatory exam, audit, or independent assessment. Internal audit Internal Audit conducts an independent review of the compliance controls in an Compliance Audit, Exam & Independent Assessment Management Program | Block 3 objective manner to ensure that we comply with the company policies, global, federal and state regulatory compliance regulations. At Block, these audits are performed by a third party, such as Deloitte, Ernst & Young on behalf of the firm. Roles & Responsibilities Compliance Core ● Communicates the importance of compliance regulatory examinations, audits, and assessments to their teams. ● Fosters a collaborative and open environment where Leads are empowered to address questions directly. ● Provides support, direction, or guidance to leads for questions that are ambiguous or unclear. Compliance Leads ● Provide cohesive responses for audit, exam, and assessment questions. ● Ensure responses to questions posed by auditors and examiners are accurate, complete and submitted to the Exam and Audit team in a timely manner. ● Escalate responses that require review by Compliance core. ● Develop comprehensive management action plans (MAPs)/ Corrective Actions that address the root cause of issues and mitigate risks to acceptable levels. EXAM Team ● Provides support for and facilitates regulatory-driven audits, exams, and assessments (excluding contractual obligations). ● Facilitates interactions and communications with auditors and examiners. ● Engages Compliance Leads for audit, exam, and assessment requests and questions. ● Reviews and provides responses to draft observations by auditor/examiner. ● Works with auditors/examiners to finalize reports. documents.GOV-032: Compliance Committee & Forum Oversight Standard Block, Inc. Last Approved: Mar 31, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 31, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Compliance Committee & Forum Oversight Standard (the “Standardˮ) outlines Block, Inc.ʼs (“Blockˮ or “the Companyˮ) documented approach to the structure and operation of Compliance governance. The purpose of this Standard is to document the requirements and principles governing the Compliance Committee and Forum Oversight Framework (the “Frameworkˮ) that was established to ensure appropriate oversight and accountability of the Compliance program. The Framework outlines the structure for a Compliance governance model that maintains clear accountability, supports effective decision-making, and ensures appropriate oversight. Scope: This Standard applies to Block, Inc., its subsidiaries, and affiliates (collectively, “Blockˮ), including all products, business lines, and jurisdictions. Further, this Standard applies to all formally governed Compliance Committees and Forums and employees participating in the governance bodies. An exception to this scope is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. Definitions: Terminology Definition Tracked Action Items Activities requiring execution or completion that arise and are agreed upon during Forum or Committee meetings. Tracked Action Items are documented, including a description of the action, the responsible party, and the expected completion timeframe. These items, which arise from Committee or Forum meetings require formal oversight, documentation, and closure verification through the governance process. Any task that does not meet this definition will not be centrally tracked, reported, or formally evidenced, and will instead be managed through standard BAU routines by the relevant program owners, stakeholders, and/or DRIs. 1 Terminology Definition Additional Observers Individuals who may attend a meeting where their participation is deemed relevant or necessary. Committee A formally constituted and authorized governance body that makes decisions, approves actions, addresses escalated risk issues, and/or provides management oversight (as documented in the Committee Charter). Committee Chair The chairperson (“Chairˮ) for a Committee meeting who, in accordance with the Committee Charter, is assigned ownership, responsibility, and accountability for the facilitation of the Committee meetings. Committee Charter A formal document that outlines and defines the Committeeʼs purpose, authority, composition, and responsibilities. The (“Charterˮ) Charter establishes the Committeeʼs role in supporting oversight of compliance with applicable laws, regulations, internal policies, and ethical standards. The Charter also defines meeting frequency, reporting requirements, and access to information to support effective communication, information, and alignment across the organization. Decision-Making The formal authority to approve actions, address escalated risk issues, and provide management oversight, which resides at Level 3 committees and above. Decisions, which require committee quorum, are documented through meeting minutes, and, as appropriate, reported upward to Level 2 Block Risk Council / ERM Issues Management) and ultimately Level 1 Board / Audit & Risk Committee). For example, the Compliance Strategy and Risk Committee CSRC and the Compliance Management System CMS Health Committee are formally constituted, Chief Compliance Officer-chaired governance bodies with voting members, chartered authority, and formal meeting minutes. They receive consolidated reporting from Level 4 forums and may make decisions on matters such as policy approvals, risk alignment, escalated issues, and programmatic changes. Delegate A temporary representative who attends a Forum or Committee meeting on behalf of a

Overview Introduction and Purpose: Block, Inc (“Blockˮ) requires employees (“Staff Membersˮ) to complete mandatory Compliance training. This Escalation Framework for Non-Completion of Compliance Training (“Frameworkˮ) defines the approach taken by Block for Staff Members who do not complete mandatory compliance training, as required. The purpose of this document is to outline the Escalation Framework for Employees who have not completed mandatory Compliance training despite undergoing the current Compliance training escalation process as outlined in the Block Compliance Mandatory Training Program Standard (the “Standardˮ). For mandatory Compliance training, the Standard outlines the end-to-end Training Campaign Cycle, which gives Learners 30 days to complete the assigned mandatory training on time, followed by a 7 day escalating communication process post-due date aimed at driving completion of compliance training. Select mandatory Compliance training may follow an adjusted timeline as outlined in the Program. This Framework takes effect for Staff Members who have not completed mandatory Compliance training at the conclusion of the Compliance Training Campaign Cycle which can be thought of as day 1 of the Consequences phase. This Framework is owned and maintained by the Compliance Training Team who partners with HR, People Ops, External Workforce, and Employment Counsel on changes to the Framework. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Global Compliance Monitoring and Testing Program (“Programˮ) outlines Blockʼs approach to Regulatory Compliance Testing and Monitoring within the Compliance organization. The GCMT Program Lead is responsible for management of this Program. Block, Inc. (herein referred to as “Blockˮ or the “Companyˮ) is committed to maintaining a robust Global Compliance Monitoring & Testing GCMT function and maintaining compliance with the laws and regulations governing the conduct of its business. As part of this commitment, Block developed this Program to define the GCMT function, its purpose, and general responsibilities. The GCMT function tests and reports on the effectiveness of internal controls so that control owners can take prompt remedial action. GCMT is part of the second line of defense in Blockʼs three lines of defense risk management model. GCMT brings a systematic, disciplined testing approach to Blockʼs oversight activities. Compliance testing serves as an independent and objective assurance activity designed to enhance Blockʼs oversight activities. GCMT is granted authority access to records, reports, systems, and personnel necessary to execute risk-based compliance monitoring and testing. The purpose of the GCMT function is to support Blockʼs risk governance framework. GCMT provides periodic monitoring and testing of compliance activities and control environments to determine whether internal controls over compliance activities are adequately designed and functioning effectively. Other approaches we can take include: ● ‘Go liveʼ Readiness Assessments ● Post-Implementation Reviews ● Consultations to evaluate the alignment between frameworks, policies, and regulations First and second line of defense employees, including Managers, Officers, and Executives, must be familiar with this Program. documents.GOV-037: Regulatory Change Management Standard Block, Inc. Last Approved: Jan 29, 2026 Effective Date: Jan 29, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Block, Inc. (“Blockˮ) conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws, rules, and regulations (“LRRsˮ). This Regulatory Change Management (“RCMˮ) Standard (“Standardˮ) is designed with the objective of ensuring consistent and effective compliance with currently applicable LRRs. To meet this objective, this Standard establishes the framework for systematically managing and adapting to Regulatory Changes that impact Blockʼs customer facing activities, services and operations (“Customer Impacting Activitiesˮ) within the jurisdictions that it operates. RCM is an end-to-end process that supports Block identifying, managing, and maintaining effective and consistent compliance with the LRRs that are applicable to its Customer Impacting Activities. This Standard is designed to establish a framework for: ● Defining the overarching guiding principles for managing Regulatory Changes ● Establishing roles and responsibilities to drive accountability for the overall Regulatory Change management process ● Tracking and monitoring enacted LRRs within the scope of RCM ● Communicating changes to LRRs to the impacted stakeholders ● Ensuring that stakeholders implement risk mitigation measures and document compliance with applicable LRRs Scope: This standard is established by Block to set out a consistent and effective approach to managing compliance with enacted LRRs applicable to its U.S. based operations. Regulatory changes include changes to existing LRRs, along with newly enacted LRRs. The following are outside the scope of RCM (i) laws applicable to proposed or contemplated products until the product is made available to customers, (ii) LRRs applicable to Blockʼs non-Customer Impacting Activities, and (iii) LRRs applicable solely to Cash App Taxes. 1 The scope of LRRs covered by this Standard will constantly change. The Regulatory Change Management team will incorporate additional LRRs to the Obligations library as it becomes aware of new or changed LRRs and new or changed Customer Impacting Activities. This Standard governs the management of go-forward regulatory changes, and does not apply to regulatory changes enacted prior to the RCM program launch date Sept 30, 2025. Obligation Any individual requirement or expectation that Block must comply with to fulfil legal and compliance responsibilities. Protecht serves as a system of record for RCM. Regulatory Change Any addition, removal, or modification of LRRs.
