documents.GOV-038: Service Level Agreement and Backlog Management Standard Block Inc. Last Approved: Mar 30, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 30, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The Block, Inc. (“Blockˮ) Service Level Agreement (“SLAˮ) and Backlog Management Standard (“Standardˮ) outlines the strategy for adherence related to SLAs. The key components of this strategy include planning and resourcing for sufficient staffing, and developing remediation plans in the event there is variance to the planning and alerts age outside the established SLA. This Policy is established by Block, Inc. (“Blockˮ) and is applicable globally across all Block business lines, entities, and jurisdictions. References to “staff members'' throughout this document shall refer to all full-time employees, fixed-term, and contingent workers (such as BPO who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Policy, and include interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. Scope: This Standard applies to Block, Inc., its entities, and affiliates (collectively, “Blockˮ), including all products. Definitions: Terminology Definition Service Level A defined timeframe within which a unit of work must be completed. This Agreement SLA refers to the allowed number of hours, calendar days, or business days to complete a review of an alert, suspicious activity report SAR investigation, or other unit of work (such as an Office of Foreign Assets Control OFAC report or partner escalation). In some cases, such as remediations, the SLA may be expressed as a specific calendar date rather than a number of days. 1 Out of SLA An alert or other unit of work (i.e. a SAR case) that has remained open past (“OOSLAˮ) the standard SLA but has not yet reached Backlog status. The Out of SLA (“OOSLAˮ) period spans from 1 day past the SLA deadline up to 2x the standard SLA. For example, if the standard SLA is 60 days, an alert is considered Out of SLA from day 61 through day 120. At day 121, the alert moves to the Backlog. Backlog An alert, SAR investigation, or other unit of work that has remained open greater than 2x the standard SLA. For example, if the standard SLA is 60 days, an alert moves to the Backlog on day 121. Internal SLA An internal SLA is determined by the Operations Lead as appropriate to the risk and complexity and is typically reflected as the due date in case management systems and metrics reporting. The SLA can range from 24 hours/1 day, 7 days, 30 days or 60 days. SLA may be measured in calendar days or business days, depending on the workflow. For certain workflows, including SSP-enabled alerts, internal SLA may operate in multiple stages, including: ● Initial alert review SLA, ● Defined customer response waiting period, and ● Follow-up adjudication SLA once the case is re-queued for analyst action. Regulatory SLA A Regulatory SLA is an external deadline set by regulators, such as filing a SAR within 30 days of determining suspicion or filing an OFAC report within 10 days. Regulatory SLAs are binary. Alert An alert is generated notification in queues within case management systems triggered by predefined rules or risk indicators that require review by Compliance Operations to determine whether further action or escalation is necessary. Queues A queue is a designated workflow within a case management system where alerts or cases are routed for review by a specific team based on predefined risk criteria or business rules. Alert queues generate alerts based on heuristics or machine learning models, or a combination of both. Escalate to SAR An Escalate to SAR action is taken when there is reason to investigate unusual activity further by a dedicated investigations team. When an Escalate to SAR action is taken and a case is generated for investigation, the internal SLA starts. SARs may also be referenced as Suspicious Transaction Report STR and Suspicious Matter Report SMR and other regional variations. SAR An investigation
documents.GOV-039: Compliance Quality Program Standard Block, Inc. Last Approved: Mar 27, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 27, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose This Compliance Quality Standard establishes a consistent framework for assessing and improving the quality and effectiveness of compliance processes across Block, Inc. and its subsidiaries (collectively, “Blockˮ). Block has designed its Quality Program to consist of two parts: Quality Control (“QC”) and Quality Assurance (“QA”). The Program ensures that Compliance Operations, with responsibility for implementing processes and procedures to meet Blockʼs BSA/AML and Sanctions policy and program as it relates to areas such as Transaction Monitoring (“TM"), Suspicious Activity Reporting (“SARˮ), Enhanced Due Diligence (“EDDˮ), and Know Your Customer (“KYCˮ) reviews, meet regulatory obligations and internal quality benchmarks. The Quality Program may be leveraged for other areas as designated, such as Appeals, Unacceptable Use and Terms of Service Violation, within the scope of the Compliance Operations program. Block is committed to maintaining a robust Quality Program and compliance with the laws and regulations governing the conduct of its business. As part of this commitment, Block developed this Standard to govern the Quality Program and define its objectives, principles, methodology, and governance, ensuring independent, accurate, and transparent evaluation of compliance reviews to strengthen Blockʼs control environment and promote continuous improvement. This Standard will be supported by procedures and instruction; separated into specific control and assurance components, or by function performing quality, where relevant. A primary function of the Quality Program is evaluation of the effectiveness of the Compliance operational functions in meeting the objectives and obligations under the BSA/AML and Sanctions programs. The Quality Program primarily focuses on output and decisions related to alerts and investigations, and provides a feedback loop for continuous improvement after a decision has been made. The Quality Control QC function primarily focuses on real-time evaluation of decision correctness either prior to a decision being made or shortly thereafter. The Quality Assurance QA function) evaluates decisions made with a systematic review of existing workflows, processes, procedures, training and where relevant, systems, to detect any weaknesses or inefficiencies that 1 could compromise quality, introduce errors that expose the compliance operations program to gaps, or hinder effectiveness of the operations program meeting its obligations. Once identified, these deficiencies are then thoroughly analyzed to understand their root causes, paving the way for targeted remediation efforts. The goal of the Quality Program is to provide recommendations and reporting that drive correction of issues as well as implementation of lasting solutions that prevent recurrence and enhance overall operational procedures and program in fulfilling its objectives under the BSA/AML and Sanctions programs. Scope This Standard applies globally to Block, Inc. entities and products, specifically those defined within Covered Products, as applicable to Square, Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Cash App, Cash App Invest (“CAIˮ) Afterpay and Clearpay, and covers all compliance-related reviews, investigations, and quality control and assurance processes. In addition to application to Blockʼs BSA/AML and Sanctions programs, the Quality Program may be leveraged for other areas as designated, such as Appeals, Unacceptable Use and Terms of Service Violation, within the scope of the Compliance Operations program. In-scope Coverage Unless otherwise explicitly stated, directed, or approved through governance channels, the following areas are in scope for this Standard and the Quality Program: ● Compliance Operations: All Compliance Operations functions as performed within queues in Regulator or Notary are in scope

Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Fitness and Propriety Standard (“Standardˮ) outlines Clearpay Finance Limitedʼs (“CFLˮ) approach to Fitness and Propriety of all staff members acting in the role of either a Significant Management Function (“SMFˮ) or Certified Function (“Certifiedˮ) under the Senior Managers and Certification Regime (“SMCRˮ), a UK Regulation under the Financial Conduct Authority (“FCAˮ). documents.GOV-043: Training and Competence Standard Clearpay Finance Ltd Last Approved: Mar 25, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 25, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Clearpay Finance Limited (“Clearpayˮ) is committed to upholding the highest standards of conduct and governance. This Training and Competence Standard (“Standardˮ) outlines Clearpayʼs approach to complying with the Financial Conduct Authority (“FCAˮ)ʼs requirements to ensure all staff possess the required competence to undertake their responsibilities to Clearpay regulated activities. The Chief Compliance Officer is responsible for management of this Standard. Scope: This Standard applies to Clearpay, all its employees, outsourced service providers, consultants, contractors, suppliers and agents (“Clearpay employeesˮ). It is the duty of all Clearpay employees to be familiar with this Standard and any operational routines concerning Training and Competence which are relevant to their work. Mandatory compliance training administered in person and through Clearpayʼs Learning Management System (“LMSˮ) is managed under this Standard. Clearpay updates its Compliance Training Calendar on an annual basis , which details the schedule and audiences for global and regional mandatory compliance training for the calendar year. Clearpay is authorised and regulated by the FCA to enter into regulated consumer credit agreements with customers (“consumersˮ or “customersˮ). Under the FCAʼs Systems and Controls requirements (“SYSCˮ) Clearpay is required to ensure it employs personnel with the skills, knowledge and expertise necessary for the discharge of the responsibilities allocated to them. Clearpay also needs to ensure its systems and controls enable it to satisfy itself of the suitability of anyone who acts for it, including an assessment of their competence. This assessment should take account of the level of responsibility of that individual. Definitions: Terminology Definition 1 Assignment Generally, enterprise-wide mandatory compliance training is assigned to new hires within a week of onboarding and they are given 30 days to complete the training. For enterprise-wide mandatory compliance training assigned annually, staff are generally given 45 days to complete the training. Exceptions are outlined in the Compliance Training Calendar. Enrollment Staff Members who are assigned training based on eligibility criteria Audience determined and approved by the Content Owner for each training course. Training The end-to-end cycle to manage a specific training course. Campaign Staff Member(s) All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Standards, and includes interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers and contractors. FCA Financial Conduct Authority Clearpay is authorised and regulated by the FCA. 2. Standard Requirements 2.1. Board Training Board Training is delivered on an annual basis to the Clearpay Board by the Chief Compliance Officer and/or Money Laundering Reporting Officer (“MLROˮ) or their delegate. This training may include but is not limited to AML, Anti-Bribery & Corruption (“ABCˮ),and Consumer Protection topics along with any additional content identified by the CCO. If a Board Director is also an employee, they will be waived from employee-level training if they attended the Director-level training. 2.2. Intake Process New training requests for in-house development are considered and prioritised on a case-by-case basis by the Clearpay Training Team (“CTTˮ). The CTT then determines the best learning solution for required training, and the order in which the request will be prioritised against the Compliance Training Calendar (“CTCˮ). For training that will require vendor support, the CTT will manage the relationship, advise on system requirements, evaluate vendor options, customise vendor content, and support use of vendor content. 2 2.3 Training Records Training records are maintained

Document Details 12 Compliance Incident Management Standard | Block 3 Confidential and Not for Distribution Definitions Terminology Definition Incident An unforeseen, unplanned or near-miss event that negatively impacts our operations, quality of services and/or function. An Incident may be caused by either (or a combination of) internal control failure(s) or an external event outside of the organization’s control. An incident is separate from but can be related to Issues a high level control improvement, actions, risks or regulatory changes, etc. Issue A deficiency, gap, or weakness in the design, performance, or operating effectiveness of a process or control or the absence of a control. Production An Engineering event impacting one or more of the following: Incidents (Non-Security 1. a significant service (e.g., payment rails, Cash App) Incident) 2. a significant number of services (e.g., refunds, reporting, & logins) 3. a significant number of customers (e.g., x% of certain customers) 4. a significant miss in service level availability (e.g., product/service is X% down) Security/Privacy A confirmed breach, policy violation, or event that threatens the Incident confidentiality, integrity, or availability of Block Information, requiring investigation and response. Any loss or unauthorized access to, or acquisition or use of, Block’s information maintained by, or on behalf of, Block, or malicious or non-malicious activities or vulnerabilities that result in unauthorized modification or damage to Block technology or systems, including the Block network or mobile applications. A non-exhaustive list of examples includes computer system intrusion, suspected or actual breaches, compromises, or other unauthorized access to Block systems, data, applications, or accounts, denial of service (DDoS) attack or an attack that prevents or impairs the authorized use of networks, systems, or applications. SEV Levels Severity levels are defined by impact (e.g., SEV 0 to SEV 4), guiding prioritization and response efforts. SEVs is shorthand for a Product Incident. For example: blocking Payroll runs or administrative tasks Triggers update to issquareup.com ● SEV 2: Disrupting non-essential operations that can reasonably be postponed during the incidents’ occurrence. For example: delaying a non-critical report ● SEV 3: Minimal disruption to non-essential operations. Cash SEV Levels: ● SEV 0: Any incident that threatens Block or Cash in any market with serious reputational, regulatory, or business harm. ● SEV 1: Any incident that significantly impacts a large group of customers or causes widespread work halting for Cash engs. ● SEV 2: An incident

Overview Introduction and Purpose: The landscape of the regulatory environment governing financial institutions in Australia is continually evolving. The ability to effectively navigate and promptly adapt to these regulatory changes is a critical requirement for Square AU Pty Ltd (“Square AUˮ) to mitigate associated legal, operational, and regulatory risks. The Regulatory Change Management Standard (the “Standardˮ) is designed to create a robust system for proactive monitoring, interpretation, and effective responses to changes in regulations. This Standard encompasses critical assessments, planning, implementation and monitoring processes to ensure Square AU is aligned with all relevant regulatory requirements. It also covers key elements such as identifying business stakeholders, assessing the impact of a regulatory change, developing plans to address changes, and actively monitoring these plans to ensure ongoing effectiveness. Scope: This Standard is established by Square AU and applicable to Covered Products offered by Square AU. This Standard is managed and maintained by the Square AU Compliance team and sets out the approach to design and execution Square AUʼs Regulatory Change Management Standard. The Standard also applies to material third-party vendors or fintech partners where regulatory change affects their activity on behalf of Square AU. Approval History 11 Incident Response Plan | Square | Square Canada Inc. 2 Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. Introduction and Context This Incident Response Plan outlines the structure and processes to ensure that Square Canada can effectively respond to disruptions in a timely manner and in accordance with the requirements of the Retail Payments Activities Act (“RPAA”). This structure supports the escalation, management, and remediation of incidents to maintain operational resilience. Square Canada will both rely upon and will feed into Block Inc.’s existing Incident and Crisis Management processes in the event of a disruption impacting our retail payment activities. The purpose of developing and implementing a localised Square Canada Incident Management structure is to ensure that the impact of the disruption to Square Canada is fully understood, informing decision making regarding the actions required to mitigate any potential or further impacts to our retail payment activities. This playbook establishes the procedures for incident management at Square Canada in alignment with Block’s global incident response protocols. The objective is to ensure a structured and efficient response to incidents impacting Square Canada while maintaining coordination with Block’s central incident response teams. 1.1 Prerequisite Reading This document does not replace Block’s existing incident plans and runbooks, rather it is to be used as an extension of existing processes to ensure Square Canada’s alignment with RPAA incident response and reporting requirements. It is recommended that Directly Responsible Individuals (“DRI”s) directly or indirectly impacted by this runbook familiarize themselves with Block's Business Continuity Plan. 1.2 Roles & Responsibilities The listed roles and responsibilities outlined here are intended to strengthen collaboration and streamline information sharing for reporting incidents under RPAA. This is not an exhaustive list, but rather a high-level guide to understand the typical workflow for RPAA-compliant incident reporting. Detailed responsibilities are further elaborated in Block’s existing incident plans.
